proposals.title: Global, regional, and national burden of cancer potentially avertable through primary prevention: a comparative risk assessment and forecasting study, 1990–2050
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) framework has meticulously quantified the mortality and disability from cancer, but its counterfactual analyses have traditionally focused on the burden of untreated disease or the impact of treatment. To inform proactive health policy, there is a critical need to systematically model the potential health gains from primary prevention strategies, such as tobacco control, vaccination, and dietary modifications, across different global contexts. This shift in focus is essential for prioritizing cost-effective interventions that can reduce future cancer incidence and mortality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a substantial and geographically heterogeneous proportion of the global cancer burden from 1990 to 2050 is attributable to modifiable risk factors, and that scaling up primary prevention could avert millions of deaths and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) by mid-century, with the greatest potential impact in low- and middle-income countries.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 comparative risk assessment framework. Using spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression and Cause of Death Ensemble modeling (CODEm), we will establish baseline cancer mortality and incidence forecasts to 2050. We will then define and model counterfactual scenarios for scaling key primary prevention interventions (e.g., tobacco taxation, HPV/HBV vaccination, air pollution reduction) to theoretical minimum risk exposure levels or defined coverage targets. The burden averted will be calculated as the difference between the forecasted baseline and the counterfactual scenario for deaths, DALYs, and prevalence, stratified by country, cancer type, sex, and age.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, long-term global estimates of the cancer burden avertable through primary prevention, offering a direct evidence base for investment in prevention. It will quantify the potential health gains by intervention and region, identifying priority areas for public health action. The results will complement existing GBD treatment-focused analyses, presenting a complete picture of cancer control strategies.
proposals.novelty: While GBD studies have quantified attributable burden, this work innovates by explicitly modeling the dynamic *avertable* burden through forward-looking, scalable intervention scenarios, rather than static attribution. It inverts the traditional counterfactual from 'burden of untreated disease' to 'burden prevented by proactive intervention,' creating a novel metric for policy planning.

proposals.title: Robustness of Global Burden of Disease Estimates to Imputation Methods: A Comparative Analysis for Low-Resource Settings, 1990–2023
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study provides critical health metrics for policy, but its estimates for low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) often rely on complex statistical models to impute missing or sparse data. The choice of imputation method—ranging from spatial-temporal Gaussian process regression to simpler country-level borrowing—can significantly influence final estimates of mortality, incidence, and prevalence, yet the sensitivity of GBD results to these methodological choices is not systematically quantified. This creates uncertainty in priority-setting for regions where accurate data is most needed but least available.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that alternative, robust imputation methods (e.g., penalized splines, hierarchical Bayesian models with weakly informative priors, and direct neighbor borrowing) will yield meaningfully different estimates of cause-specific mortality and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) for select cancers and infectious diseases in data-sparse regions compared to the standard GBD modeling suite, with the divergence being largest for causes with the most fragmented data.
proposals.method: Using the GBD 2023 data infrastructure, we will conduct a sensitivity analysis focusing on 3-5 high-burden, data-variable conditions (e.g., cervical cancer, malaria, chronic kidney disease). For countries classified as data-sparse in GBD 2023, we will substitute the standard DisMod-MR 2.1 and CODEm models with three alternative imputation frameworks: 1) Bayesian hierarchical models with regularization priors to shrink towards regional averages, 2) multivariate imputation by chained equations (MICE) incorporating ancillary health system covariates, and 3) a simple geospatial kriging approach independent of the GBD causal architecture. We will compare resulting national-level estimates for 2023 and evaluate trends from 1990-2023.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, condition-specific map of estimation uncertainty attributable to imputation choices within the GBD framework. It will produce practical guidance on which burden estimates are most robust to methodological changes and which are highly sensitive, thereby informing users and funders about the reliability of rankings and trends. The analysis will also propose a set of diagnostic metrics for ongoing evaluation of model dependence in future GBD rounds.
proposals.novelty: While GBD studies extensively model uncertainty from data sampling, they do not formally isolate and quantify the uncertainty introduced by the specific choice of imputation and smoothing algorithms in data-sparse contexts. This proposal systematically deconstructs that component of model variance, moving beyond validation to a foundational assessment of estimand stability.

proposals.title: Global, regional, and national burden of oral diseases, 1990–2030: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2025 to monitor progress towards the WHO 2030 oral health target
proposals.motivation: In 2022, the World Health Organization (WHO) established a global target to reduce the burden of oral diseases by 10% by 2030. However, comprehensive, standardized, and geographically granular monitoring of progress towards this target is lacking, hindering effective policy evaluation and resource allocation. This study addresses a critical gap in global health surveillance by providing a rigorous, data-driven assessment of whether global oral health efforts are on track.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that global progress towards the WHO 2030 oral health target will be heterogeneous, with significant disparities across regions and countries, and that current trends from 2021 onward will be insufficient to achieve the 10% reduction goal at the global level.
proposals.method: We will extend the Global Burden of Diseases, Injuries, and Risk Factors (GBD) Study framework to model the burden of key oral conditions (e.g., untreated caries, severe periodontitis, edentulism) from 1990 to 2023 and forecast to 2030. Using established GBD spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression and Cause of Death Ensemble modeling (CODEm) architectures, we will synthesize all available data from surveys, administrative sources, and scientific literature. We will then calculate annualized rates of change from 2021 to 2030 at global, regional, and national levels to compare projected trends against the WHO target trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, quantitative assessment of progress towards the WHO 2030 oral health target, providing benchmark estimates for 204 countries and territories. It will identify geographical areas and specific oral conditions where current interventions are failing, thereby offering an evidence base for course correction in global oral health policy and investment.
proposals.novelty: While the GBD study has previously estimated oral disease burden, this proposal uniquely integrates these estimates with the formal WHO 2030 target to create a forward-looking policy-monitoring tool, applying advanced forecasting methods standard in GBD cancer and mortality analyses to the under-modeled domain of oral health.

proposals.title: The global and national burden of oral conditions attributable to shared risk factors with diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and cancers: a comparative risk assessment for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2025
proposals.motivation: Oral conditions, such as severe periodontitis and untreated caries, are recognized as major non-communicable diseases (NCDs) with substantial global prevalence and disability. Pre-2026 evidence indicates strong epidemiological links with systemic NCDs like diabetes, but these conditions remain siloed in global health metrics and policy. Quantifying the shared risk factor burden is critical to advocate for integrated prevention strategies within universal health coverage frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant and growing proportion of the global burden (deaths, YLLs, and YLDs) from oral conditions is attributable to modifiable risk factors—specifically high fasting plasma glucose, smoking, and dietary risks—that are also leading drivers of diabetes, cardiovascular diseases, and certain cancers.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2025 comparative risk assessment framework. Using GBD 2021 estimates for oral condition prevalence, incidence, and mortality as a baseline, we will model population-attributable fractions for selected risk factors using consistent meta-analytical tools (DisMod-MR 2.1, Bayesian meta-regression). The analysis will be conducted at global, regional, and national levels from 1990 to 2025, employing spatial-temporal Gaussian process regression to address data gaps. Results will be forecast to 2050 using cohort projections of risk factor exposure.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first comprehensive, quantified estimates of the oral disease burden driven by major shared NCD risks within the GBD architecture. It will provide actionable evidence for cross-cutting NCD policy and identify priority populations where integrated interventions could yield the greatest co-benefits for oral and systemic health.
proposals.novelty: While prior GBD studies have quantified oral disease burden and risk factors separately, this analysis novelly integrates oral conditions into the core NCD risk-death pathway model, explicitly quantifying the overlapping etiological burden with other major NCDs at a global scale.

proposals.title: Integrating Digital Epidemiology with GBD Frameworks: A Real-Time Sentinel System for Cancer Burden and Risk Factor Surveillance
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study provides comprehensive but lagged estimates of cancer burden, with final data typically 2-3 years behind the present. This latency limits timely public health response to emerging trends in cancer incidence or shifts in population-level risk factors. The proliferation of digital data sources, such as aggregated search queries and social media, offers a potential solution for near-real-time surveillance, but their integration into formal burden estimation frameworks remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: That validated, high-frequency digital signals (e.g., Google Trends for symptom searches, Twitter/X data for behavioral mentions) can be statistically fused with traditional GBD inputs to generate accurate, real-time nowcasts of cancer-relevant outcomes and risk factor prevalence at national and subnational levels.
proposals.method: We will employ a Bayesian hierarchical modeling architecture, extending established GBD spatio-temporal models. For specific cancer sites (e.g., lung, colorectal), we will curate digital proxies (search term volumes for symptoms, smoking cessation app downloads) and link them to historical GBD estimates (1990-2023) to train the model. The core algorithm will be an ensemble of penalized spline regressions and Gaussian process models, dynamically weighting digital and traditional data streams based on their predictive validity in held-back years. Validation will be performed against the most recent GBD data releases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, replicable methodological pipeline for integrating digital epidemiology into the GBD ecosystem, enabling near-real-time monitoring of cancer burden signals. It will deliver proof-of-concept nowcasts for selected cancers and risk factors, providing a template for expanding digital sentinel surveillance to other disease categories within the GBD study.
proposals.novelty: While digital epidemiology has been used for infectious disease tracking, its systematic integration into the comprehensive, cause-of-death-based GBD framework for non-communicable diseases like cancer is novel. The proposed method moves beyond simple correlation to formal statistical fusion within an established burden estimation paradigm.

proposals.title: An agent-based model of cancer burden to 2050: simulating non-linear interactions of risk factor exposure, demographic change, and healthcare access
proposals.motivation: Current forecasts of global cancer burden, such as those from GBD 2023, rely on statistical extrapolations of past trends, which may not capture complex, non-linear interactions between individual-level risk factors, demographic shifts, and heterogeneous healthcare access. As countries undergo rapid epidemiological transitions, understanding how these interacting dynamics could alter future cancer incidence and mortality is critical for proactive, targeted health system planning and policy intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that agent-based modeling (ABM) will reveal significant deviations from linear forecast trends for specific cancer types by 2050, particularly in transitioning economies, due to the simulation of dynamic interactions between individual behaviors, risk accumulation, and access to screening and treatment.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-time, stochastic ABM using a modified ODD (Overview, Design concepts, Details) protocol. Agents, representing individuals, will be initialized with demographic attributes (age, sex, location) and risk profiles (smoking, BMI, alcohol) derived from GBD 2023 and STEPs data. The model will incorporate known biological rules for cancer onset (e.g., cumulative hazard functions) and progression. Agents will interact with a simulated healthcare environment parameterized with GBD Healthcare Access and Quality (HAQ) Index data, influencing screening probabilities and stage-specific survival. The model will be calibrated to historical GBD estimates (1990-2023) using approximate Bayesian computation (ABC) and then run forward under alternative policy and risk factor scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, mechanistic simulation tool for global cancer forecasting that can test the impact of combined prevention and treatment scenarios. It will generate spatially explicit, subnational forecasts that account for individual heterogeneity and system interactions, providing a more nuanced evidence base for resource allocation than traditional aggregate models.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs are used in communicable disease and health policy, their application to long-term, global cancer forecasting at the individual level is novel. This approach moves beyond correlational forecasting to explicitly model the mechanistic pathways from risk exposure to disease outcome within a simulated population.

proposals.title: A Bayesian small-area estimation framework for community-level cancer burden: informing targeted prevention and screening in King County, Washington, USA
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study provides critical national and subnational estimates, but health system planning often occurs at the community or neighborhood level where risk factors and healthcare access vary dramatically. Existing GBD methodologies are not routinely applied at scales finer than the first administrative subdivision, creating a gap between population health metrics and local public health action, particularly for cancer control.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting GBD's cause-of-death ensemble modeling and DisMod-MR 2.1 to incorporate high-resolution, community-level covariates and multi-source data, we can generate robust estimates of cancer incidence, prevalence, and mortality for small geographic units, revealing inequities and intervention opportunities masked by larger-area estimates.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD small-area estimation framework to census tract levels in King County. Using Bayesian hierarchical models, we will synthesize data from state cancer registries, vital statistics, health surveys, and electronic health records. Spatial and temporal Gaussian Process regressions will smooth estimates, incorporating covariates like area deprivation index, screening facility density, and environmental exposures. Model validation will use cross-validation and comparison with held-out registry data.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first validated, comprehensive estimates of cancer burden at a community scale using GBD principles, directly linking global comparative risk assessment to local planning. It will provide a replicable methodological template for other jurisdictions and demonstrate how subnational burden studies can be operationalized for precision public health.
proposals.novelty: While GBD has produced subnational analyses for entire countries, the systematic downscaling of the full causal and risk-attributable burden framework to the community level for direct intervention planning is a novel application. The integration of non-traditional, high-granularity data sources into the established GBD modeling architecture represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Nowcasting Global and Subnational All-Cause and Cancer Mortality by Integrating Real-Time Digital Trace Data with Traditional Vital Statistics: A Modelling Study
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study provides comprehensive mortality estimates but operates on a multi-year lag, limiting its utility for timely policy response during health crises or rapid demographic shifts. Pre-2026, the proliferation of non-traditional, high-frequency data streams (e.g., internet search trends, social media activity, mobility data) offers a potential solution, but their systematic integration into formal mortality estimation frameworks remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Bayesian hierarchical nowcasting model, which dynamically weights multiple real-time digital data streams against traditional lagged mortality data, can produce accurate, subnational estimates of all-cause and cancer mortality with a latency reduction of 12-18 months compared to standard GBD cycles.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD's cause-of-death ensemble model (CODEm) framework into a dynamic nowcasting architecture. For selected countries with high-quality vital registration (e.g., Japan, the US), we will train a model on historical (1990-2019) data, linking traditional mortality covariates with temporally aligned digital proxies (e.g., Google Trends for symptom searches, Apple Mobility data, funeral-related web traffic). A recurrent neural network (RNN) layer will model temporal dependencies in the digital streams. For the nowcast period (2020-2025), a Bayesian pooling model will integrate predictions from the digital-proxy model with gradually incorporating, incomplete official death counts to produce posterior estimates with uncertainty intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated methodological pipeline for producing more contemporaneous burden of disease estimates, directly addressing the latency challenge in global health metrics. It will deliver a proof-of-concept, subnational nowcast of all-cause and cancer mortality for 2020-2025, quantifying the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequent recovery with reduced lag.
proposals.novelty: While digital proxies have been used for disease surveillance (e.g., influenza), their integration into the formal, complex Bayesian machinery of the GBD for comprehensive mortality nowcasting, particularly for chronic diseases like cancer, is novel. The approach moves beyond correlation to establish a dynamic calibration model between digital traces and final mortality statistics.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the global burden of cancer: A comparative analysis of Years of Life Lost using location-specific versus standard life expectancy
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study calculates Years of Life Lost (YLL) using a standard life expectancy derived from the highest observed national life tables. This approach, while enabling global comparability, may misrepresent the true mortality burden within specific populations, as it does not account for the prevailing survival expectations in different countries or demographic cohorts. For diseases like cancer, where risk and mortality vary significantly by geography and age, a more nuanced YLL metric could better inform local and regional health priority setting and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calculating YLLs for major cancers using location-specific life expectancy (LSLE) and cohort-specific life expectancy (CSLE) will produce substantially different, and more locally relevant, burden estimates compared to the standard GBD approach, particularly in regions with lower overall life expectancy.
proposals.method: We will leverage the GBD 2023 cancer estimates and life tables. For the LSLE approach, we will calculate YLLs for each location (country and subnational where available) using its corresponding GBD life table. For the CSLE approach, we will use period life tables specific to the age, sex, and location of each death. We will compare these results against the standard GBD YLLs (using the global standard life table) for all cancers and major cancer types from 1990-2023. Statistical analysis will quantify absolute and relative differences, and we will produce novel maps and time-series plots of the YLL ratio (LSLE/Standard) to visualize geographic and temporal disparities.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, global assessment of how alternative life expectancy standards reshape the quantified burden of cancer. It will generate a novel dataset of location- and cohort-sensitive YLLs, offering policymakers a more contextualized view of premature mortality. The findings will directly inform methodological debates within comparative risk assessment and burden of disease studies.
proposals.novelty: While the theoretical limitation of a single standard life expectancy is known, a systematic, empirical quantification of its effect on global cancer burden—using the GBD's own granular data and proposing practical, implementable alternatives (LSLE/CSLE)—has not been previously published at this scale.

proposals.title: Global and national determinants of high mean age at death: a decomposition analysis of survival gains, 1990–2023
proposals.motivation: Global health metrics, such as the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study, have traditionally focused on quantifying causes of premature mortality and years of life lost (YLL). This deficit-based framework identifies what reduces lifespan but provides less systematic insight into the drivers of successful longevity. Understanding the specific contributions of disease reduction, injury prevention, and health system factors to achieving a high mean age at death is crucial for setting positive, actionable health policy targets beyond merely averting loss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gains in mean age at death are primarily driven by reductions in mortality from non-communicable diseases (NCDs) in older age groups, but that the relative contribution of communicable, maternal, neonatal, and nutritional (CMNN) disease reductions varies substantially by national Socio-demographic Index (SDI), with health system access and quality mediating these relationships.
proposals.method: We will leverage the complete GBD 2023 mortality and cause-of-death estimates for all ages, sexes, countries, and years from 1990–2023. The primary outcome will be the population mean age at death. We will apply a decomposition analysis using a continuous-change demographic method to partition changes in mean age at death into contributions from specific causes (e.g., cardiovascular diseases, cancers, chronic respiratory diseases, injuries) and age groups. This will be complemented by spatiotemporal Bayesian meta-regression models to quantify associations between changes in mean age at death and covariates like SDI, healthcare access and quality index (HAQ), and specific risk factor exposures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, cause-decomposed account of global survival gains, shifting the narrative from 'avoiding death' to 'achieving longevity.' It will produce policy-relevant metrics identifying which causes and age groups have contributed most to increased mean age at death for each country and SDI quintile. The findings will directly inform life-course health strategies and resource allocation for aging populations.
proposals.novelty: While extensive literature models life expectancy and YLL, systematically modeling mean age at death as a positive outcome and decomposing its drivers across the full age-cause spectrum represents a novel inversion of the standard burden-of-disease paradigm. This moves beyond forecasting cancer mortality or malaria incidence to actively modeling the attainment of long life.

proposals.title: Bayesian Small-Area Estimation of Subnational All-Cause and Cancer Mortality: A Global Comparative Analysis, 2000–2024
proposals.motivation: Subnational mortality estimates are critical for equitable health policy and resource allocation, yet many low- and middle-income countries lack reliable, granular vital registration data. Pre-2026, the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) studies have advanced subnational assessments, but persistent data sparsity in many regions limits the precision and comparability of local mortality rates, particularly for causes like cancer where risk factors and outcomes vary sharply within countries.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Bayesian hierarchical models from spatial demography, which explicitly model spatial autocorrelation and borrow strength across areas and time, will yield more precise and robust subnational mortality estimates than current GBD methods in data-sparse settings, reducing uncertainty and revealing previously obscured local disparities.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the Besag-York-Mollié (BYM2) model, a standard in spatial demography, within an integrated nested Laplace approximation (INLA) framework. The model will integrate multiple data sources: subnational vital registration (where available), sample registration systems, demographic surveys, and census data. Covariates (e.g., satellite-derived night-time lights, healthcare access) will inform priors in a space-time structure, allowing estimates for all second administrative level units globally from 2000–2024, with cross-validation used to compare performance against existing GBD life tables.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first globally consistent, high-resolution dataset of subnational all-cause and cancer mortality rates for 2000–2024, directly informing the GBD's subnational life table estimation. It will provide a validated methodological framework for incorporating spatial smoothing into the GBD's ensemble modeling approach, improving estimates where data are scarce.
proposals.novelty: While spatial smoothing is used in disease-specific mapping (e.g., malaria), its systematic integration into the core demographic estimation of all-cause and cancer mortality for the GBD's subnational life tables represents a novel synthesis of spatial demography and comprehensive burden of disease methodology.

proposals.title: Disaggregating global and national cancer burden forecasts to 2050 by sociodemographic strata: a comparative risk assessment of race, ethnicity, income, and education
proposals.motivation: National and subnational burden of disease forecasts, while crucial for policy, mask profound health inequities. Prior to 2026, the GBD and similar studies have primarily reported at the population level, obscuring the differential risk and future trajectories for marginalized groups. Identifying which sociodemographic subgroups are projected to bear the highest future cancer burden is essential for designing targeted, equitable prevention and control strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that disaggregating existing GBD 2023 cancer forecasts by race, ethnicity, income, and education will reveal divergent trends, with marginalized subgroups experiencing significantly higher future mortality and years of life lost (YLLs) rates compared to national averages, even within the same geographic units.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 cancer forecasting models by integrating sociodemographic data as stratification variables. Using a Bayesian meta-regression framework (DisMod-MR 3.0) and spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression, we will model incidence, prevalence, and mortality rates for key cancers. Subgroup data will be sourced from linked census, survey, and vital registration microdata, and disparities will be quantified using the population attributable fraction (PAF) methodology from comparative risk assessment. Forecasts to 2050 will be generated for each subgroup within countries and states.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first globally comprehensive, long-term forecasts of cancer burden stratified by multiple sociodemographic dimensions. It will directly quantify the inequality in future cancer risk, providing a critical evidence base for equity-focused health policy. The methodological framework will establish a template for disaggregating other GBD cause forecasts.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has described historical disparities, this proposal innovates by forecasting them decades into the future using established GBD models, and by simultaneously modeling the intersection of four key sociodemographic axes at a global scale, moving beyond single-country or single-dimension analyses.

proposals.title: Subnational mapping of cancer risk factor burden in 20 high-burden countries: a Bayesian small-area estimation analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2025
proposals.motivation: National-level risk factor estimates mask critical within-country disparities, limiting the ability of health ministries to target interventions effectively. The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study has pioneered subnational analyses for all-cause mortality and disease burden, but a systematic, high-resolution mapping of key cancer risk factors (e.g., smoking, alcohol use, high BMI) is lacking. Producing granular estimates is essential for precision public health, especially in large, heterogeneous countries where risk exposure varies dramatically by region.
proposals.hypothesis: Leveraging expanded subnational survey data and spatial-temporal Gaussian Process regression models can produce robust, policy-relevant estimates of cancer risk factor exposure and attributable burden at a second administrative level (e.g., states, provinces) for at least 20 countries, revealing inequalities invisible in national averages.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD Bayesian meta-regression framework (DisMod-MR 3.0) and spatial-temporal Gaussian Process regression models used in prior malaria mapping. For selected countries with sufficient Demographic and Health Surveys (DHS), STEPwise surveys, and census data, we will model risk factor prevalence (smoking, alcohol, diet, obesity) at the most granular administrative unit available. The models will incorporate geospatial covariates (e.g., night-time lights, travel time to cities) and use country-level random walks and spatial smoothing to borrow strength across units and time, producing estimates with uncertainty intervals for 1990-2025.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first internally consistent, subnational dataset of cancer risk factor exposure and attributable burden for a major set of GBD countries, directly informing national cancer control plans. It will advance methodological standards for small-area estimation of risk factors within the GBD framework and provide a replicable template for other disease categories.
proposals.novelty: While GBD has produced subnational all-cause and cause-specific burden estimates, applying its complex risk-attribution machinery to produce granular, comparable risk factor estimates across multiple countries represents a significant scaling of methodological ambition. The integration of diverse survey microdata with spatial smoothing at this resolution for a comprehensive set of cancer risks is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Global and national trends in risk-attributable cancer burden, 1990–2024: an analysis of recent mortality and disability shifts following the COVID-19 pandemic
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) 2021 study provided a comprehensive assessment of risk-attributable burden, but the period post-2021 is critical for understanding the impact of major global disruptions, particularly the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequent health system recovery, on established risk factor trends. Quantifying how these events have altered the trajectory of key cancer risk factors (e.g., smoking, high BMI, air pollution) is essential for validating and updating global cancer control strategies and forecasts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pace of change in risk-attributable cancer mortality and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) accelerated or diverged from pre-2021 trends in the period 2021–2024, with significant heterogeneity across countries and risk factors due to differential pandemic impacts and policy responses.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD comparative risk assessment framework to incorporate mortality and exposure data through 2024. Using spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression and Cause of Death Ensemble modeling (CODEm), we will estimate deaths and DALYs for major cancers. Risk-attributable burden will be calculated using population attributable fractions based on updated exposure estimates from sources like ST-GPR and relative risks from meta-analyses. Trends from 1990–2021 will be compared to 2021–2024 changes at global, regional, and national levels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, global assessment of how risk factor impacts on cancer burden have evolved in the immediate post-pandemic years, offering crucial empirical evidence to test the accuracy of prior forecasts. It will identify which risk factors and geographic regions are experiencing the most significant trend changes, informing priority-setting for national and international cancer prevention programs.
proposals.novelty: While GBD 2021 analyses ended in 2021, this work leverages newly available data to analyze the volatile 2021–2024 period, directly testing the stability of long-term risk trends against a major global shock and providing a timely update to cancer burden attribution.

proposals.title: Inverse Burden Projection: Determining the Exposure Reductions Required to Achieve Global Cancer Mortality Targets for 2040
proposals.motivation: Current Global Burden of Disease (GBD) models excel at forecasting future disease burden based on current and projected risk factor exposures. However, policymakers need to know the specific exposure reductions required to meet concrete health targets, such as the WHO's aspirational goals for cancer control. Existing forward-projection methods do not systematically solve this inverse problem, leaving a critical gap in translating targets into actionable intervention plans.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting established GBD comparative risk assessment (CRA) frameworks, it is possible to computationally solve for the future distributions of key risk factor exposures (e.g., smoking, alcohol use, high BMI) that would be necessary to achieve predefined global and regional targets for cancer mortality and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) by 2040.
proposals.method: We will adapt the GBD 2023 CRA framework for major cancers (lung, colorectal, stomach, liver, breast). Using a Bayesian hierarchical model, we will treat target burden (e.g., a 30% reduction in age-standardized mortality rates) as a fixed input. The model will then iteratively solve for the posterior distributions of exposure levels (prevalence and intensity) for modifiable risks that satisfy this target, given constraints from demographic forecasts and non-modifiable risk trends. Computational solving will employ Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling within a defined solution space, leveraging the existing meta-regression tool, DisMod-MR.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global and regional 'exposure roadmaps' quantifying the necessary reductions in smoking, alcohol consumption, and other key factors to meet cancer burden targets. It will provide a novel, target-driven modeling tool for the GBD suite, shifting the analytical paradigm from 'what will happen' to 'what must be done' to achieve specific public health goals.
proposals.novelty: While forward-projection models are standard, inverting the CRA framework to solve for required exposures as a function of a burden target is a novel, non-trivial computational and epidemiological challenge not yet addressed in the pre-2026 GBD literature.

proposals.title: The global burden of disease averted: a comparative assessment of healthy life year gains from the hypothetical elimination of major risk factors, 1990–2023
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study has quantified the attributable burden of numerous risk factors, but these estimates represent the current, partial-attributable fraction. A complementary, policy-critical question is the total potential health gain from the complete removal of a single risk factor. This 'dual problem' provides a unified metric—healthy life years (DALYs averted)—to compare the maximum theoretical impact of different prevention strategies, informing global health priority-setting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ranking risk factors by the total healthy life years gained from their hypothetical elimination will produce a substantively different priority list than ranking by current attributable burden, due to non-linear interactions, competing risks, and demographic shifts.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 comparative risk assessment framework. For each risk factor (e.g., smoking, high systolic blood pressure, ambient particulate matter), we will calculate a counterfactual scenario of theoretical minimum risk exposure (zero exposure) globally from 1990 to 2023. Using GBD cause-of-death and disability models, we will compute age-, sex-, year-, and location-specific DALYs averted. Key steps include modeling dynamic population aging and cause-deletion life table methods to account for competing mortality risks, ensuring gains are not overestimated.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, comparable estimates of the maximum potential health benefit from eliminating individual risks globally and for all GBD regions. It will deliver a novel 'benefit elimination' ranking to complement existing 'attributable burden' rankings, offering a forward-looking tool for resource allocation and long-term strategic planning in disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While previous work estimates attributable burden, this study systematically models the *counterfactual gain* from complete risk removal for all major GBD risks simultaneously, integrating demographic feedback and competing risks to produce a novel, policy-oriented metric of preventive potential.

proposals.title: Modelling the global transition of risk factor-attributable burden across the development continuum: a forecasting analysis from 1990 to 2050
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) framework has quantified the impact of major risk factors, but a systematic, forward-looking model of how these risk profiles evolve with economic and demographic development is lacking. Understanding the predictable sequences of risk factor transitions—from communicable, maternal, and nutritional risks to behavioral and metabolic risks—is critical for proactive, evidence-based health policy in rapidly developing nations and for anticipating future global disease patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that national trajectories of risk factor-attributable burden follow predictable, non-linear patterns along a composite development continuum, and that these patterns can be modelled to produce robust, subnational forecasts of future attributable mortality and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs).
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 comparative risk assessment framework by constructing a hierarchical Bayesian model that relates age-standardized risk-attributable death and DALY rates to a latent development index (synthesizing Socio-demographic Index, urbanization, and education). Using GBD 1990–2023 data for 204 countries and territories, we will model transition curves for 20 key risk factors, accounting for country-level random effects and temporal autocorrelation. The fitted model will project risk-attributable burden to 2050 under reference scenarios, leveraging shared socioeconomic pathway (SSP) projections for the development index.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, quantifiable 'roadmap' of risk factor transitions, providing policymakers with a tool to anticipate the evolving disease burden in their populations. It will directly inform the GBD forecasting enterprise and offer novel insights into the equity and pace of the epidemiological transition across different global contexts.
proposals.novelty: While past work has described historical associations, this study innovates by formally modelling the *dynamic transition process* itself as a function of development, enabling probabilistic forecasting of future risk-attributable burden at a granular level, which moves beyond cross-sectional or simple linear extrapolation.

proposals.title: Estimating the global and national burden of cancer among forcibly displaced populations: a comparative analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2025
proposals.motivation: Forcibly displaced populations, including refugees and internally displaced persons, face heightened exposure to cancer risk factors and severe barriers to healthcare, yet their specific disease burden remains largely invisible in global health metrics. Existing Global Burden of Disease (GBD) estimates, while comprehensive at national levels, often lack the granularity to capture the distinct epidemiological profiles and mortality risks of these marginalized subpopulations. Quantifying this burden is critical for equitable health policy and targeted humanitarian response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the age-standardized cancer incidence, prevalence, and mortality rates among forcibly displaced populations are significantly higher than those of the host country populations, and that the leading cancer types differ due to distinct risk exposures and access to screening and treatment.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD comparative risk assessment framework by creating a bespoke demographic and exposure model for forcibly displaced populations. Using pre-2026 UNHCR and IDMC data, we will construct population estimates by age, sex, and country of asylum/origin from 1990-2024. We will synthesize available cohort and cross-sectional studies on cancer in these groups to estimate relative risks for key risk factors (e.g., infections, smoking). These will be integrated into the GBD's DisMod-MR 2.1 and Cause of Death Ensemble modeling (CODEm) architectures to produce estimates of cancer burden, with uncertainty, for this subpopulation, benchmarked against host-country GBD estimates.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first systematic, global estimates of cancer burden for forcibly displaced populations, disaggregated by cancer type, age, sex, and displacement context. It will directly inform the health programming of humanitarian agencies and national health systems hosting displaced persons. The methodological framework will also serve as a prototype for burden estimation in other high-risk, data-sparse subpopulations.
proposals.novelty: While GBD analyses are routinely subnational, this proposal innovates by defining the unit of analysis not by geography but by a vulnerable demographic status that crosses borders, requiring novel data integration and modeling of mobile populations within the established GBD architecture.

proposals.title: A competing-risks and multimorbidity framework for forecasting cause-specific mortality: a global, national, and subnational analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study
proposals.motivation: Current GBD mortality forecasts, including for cancer, rely on the assumption of independent cause-specific mortality, which is biologically implausible given the high prevalence of multimorbidity and shared risk factors. This limitation may lead to biased forecasts, particularly for aging populations where competing risks and disease interactions significantly shape mortality patterns. Addressing this gap is critical for improving the accuracy of health policy planning and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a formal competing-risks model and accounting for multimorbidity interactions will produce significantly different, and more plausible, forecasts of cause-specific mortality at global, national, and subnational levels compared to the standard independent-cause approach.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD forecasting framework by implementing a continuous-time multi-state model, treating major non-communicable diseases (e.g., cancers, cardiovascular diseases, diabetes) as competing risks. Using individual-level data from linked cohorts and administrative records (where available) and aggregated GBD 2023 estimates, we will employ parametric survival models (e.g., Weibull) with cause-specific hazards. Interactions will be modeled via hazard ratios for disease pairs derived from meta-analyses of pre-2026 literature. The model will be fitted using maximum likelihood estimation and validated via out-of-sample forecasting for 1990-2023.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first internally consistent, global-scale mortality forecasting model that explicitly accounts for competing risks and multimorbidity. It will deliver a new set of forecasts for major causes of death, with quantified uncertainty, and an open-source software tool for the research community to apply the framework.
proposals.novelty: While competing-risks models are established in biostatistics, their application to comprehensive, long-term global burden forecasting across all major disease categories and geographies represents a significant methodological advance beyond the status quo of the GBD and other major forecasting consortia.

proposals.title: A dynamic systems approach to modelling the co-evolution of the Socio-demographic Index and risk factor exposure: implications for global burden of disease forecasts
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) framework has traditionally treated the Socio-demographic Index (SDI) and major risk factor exposures as independent covariates in models of mortality and disease burden. This assumption is increasingly recognized as a simplification, as SDI (a composite of income, education, and fertility) directly influences and is influenced by population-level exposures like smoking, diet, and air pollution through complex developmental and policy pathways. Ignoring this dynamic feedback risks misattributing burden and producing biased long-term forecasts, particularly for cancers and other non-communicable diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modelling SDI and key risk factor exposures as dynamically co-evolving variables within a country's developmental trajectory will significantly improve the explanatory power and forecast accuracy of burden of disease models compared to models treating them as independent.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD comparative risk assessment framework by developing a coupled dynamic panel model. Using GBD 2023 data for 204 countries and territories from 1990–2023, we will model the temporal relationship between SDI and exposures (e.g., tobacco use, high BMI, ambient particulate matter) using vector autoregression (VAR) or structural equation modelling (SEM) with country-level random effects. The model will estimate bidirectional lagged effects, allowing SDI to predict future exposure levels and vice-versa. Forecasts to 2050 will be generated by jointly simulating the trajectories of SDI and risk exposures, which will then feed into cause-specific mortality models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more realistic, integrated model of how development and risk profiles jointly evolve, directly informing the methodological core of the GBD study. It will yield novel insights into the drivers of epidemiological transition, particularly for cancer, and produce revised, more robust global and national burden forecasts that account for these interdependencies.
proposals.novelty: While previous GBD analyses have used SDI as a covariate, this proposal explicitly models the bidirectional, temporal feedback between SDI and risk exposures as a dynamic system, moving beyond static correlation to model co-evolution.

proposals.title: Forecasting the global and national burden of cancer under scenarios of technological and medical innovation, 2025–2050: a modelling study for the Global Burden of Disease Study
proposals.motivation: Current GBD forecasts, such as those for cancer to 2050, primarily extrapolate past trends in risk factor exposure and demographic shifts. They inadequately account for the potential disruptive impact of emerging technologies like next-generation immunotherapies, multi-cancer early detection (MCED) tests, and AI-driven diagnostics. To inform strategic health resource allocation and R&D investment, policymakers need quantitative estimates of how such innovations could alter future mortality and disability-adjusted life year (DALY) trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating plausible, evidence-based scenarios for the diffusion and efficacy of key technological innovations into the GBD forecasting framework will produce significantly different—and likely more optimistic—projections of the global cancer burden compared to baseline trend-based forecasts.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD cancer forecasting model by integrating a scenario-based discrete-time microsimulation framework. For specific innovations (e.g., MCED for selected cancers, CAR-T cell therapies for solid tumors), we will define parameters for efficacy (mortality reduction), diffusion rate (coverage by country and healthcare access index), and cost/feasibility constraints based on pre-2026 clinical trial data and technology adoption literature. Alternative scenarios (optimistic, pessimistic, reference) will be modeled using a Bayesian hierarchical model to propagate uncertainty, generating posterior distributions of deaths, prevalence, and DALYs for 204 countries from 2025 to 2050.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, quantitative assessment of how medical technology could reshape the future landscape of cancer burden globally and nationally. It will deliver a novel, flexible modelling tool for the GBD suite to evaluate health impact scenarios for other disease areas. The results will offer crucial evidence for health technology assessment and long-term planning by entities like the WHO and national cancer institutes.
proposals.novelty: While GBD forecasts are well-established, this work innovates by formally integrating scenario modelling of discrete technological breakthroughs into its core demographic and risk-factor framework, moving beyond purely epidemiological trend extrapolation. It applies methods from technology foresight and health economics to burden of disease projections in a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Ensemble forecasting of global cancer mortality: a multi-model approach to quantify uncertainty in burden projections to 2050
proposals.motivation: Long-term health forecasts, such as the GBD cancer forecasts to 2050, are critical for policy and planning but are subject to substantial uncertainty from model specification, risk factor trajectories, and demographic shifts. Current burden forecasting typically relies on single best-fit models or scenario analysis, which may inadequately characterize the full range of plausible future outcomes. Quantifying this uncertainty robustly is essential for creating resilient health strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a multi-model ensemble forecasting approach, adapted from numerical weather prediction, will produce more reliable and better-calibrated uncertainty intervals for global and national cancer mortality projections than any single constituent model.
proposals.method: We will develop an ensemble of diverse forecasting models for cancer mortality, including Bayesian hierarchical models (like those used in GBD), ARIMA-type time-series models, and machine learning models (e.g., gradient boosting) incorporating GBD risk factor and demographic data. These models will be run for multiple cancer sites across all countries from 1990-2023, with out-of-sample validation. Ensemble weights will be determined via Bayesian model averaging and stacking methods, optimizing probabilistic calibration. The final ensemble forecast to 2050 will provide full predictive distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, reproducible framework for quantifying uncertainty in global burden of disease forecasts, moving beyond deterministic scenarios. It will deliver a publicly available ensemble of cancer mortality projections with improved probabilistic calibration, offering policymakers a clearer understanding of risk ranges. The methodology will be generalizable to other disease forecasts within the GBD framework.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods are standard in climate and economic forecasting, their systematic application to quantify uncertainty in detailed cause-specific burden of disease projections is novel. This approach moves beyond comparing independent model results to formally combining them into a single, probabilistically coherent forecast.

proposals.title: The global macroeconomic burden of antimicrobial resistance: an integrated health and economic modelling study, 1990–2024
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a critical global health threat, with pre-2026 burden estimates from studies like the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) quantifying substantial mortality and disability. However, the full societal impact remains incompletely captured, as these health burden estimates are rarely integrated with macroeconomic consequences such as productivity losses, healthcare expenditure surges, and impacts on human capital. A comprehensive, spatially detailed assessment of AMR's economic cost is urgently needed to inform priority-setting and investment in countermeasures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating spatially and temporally resolved AMR health burden estimates with macroeconomic models will reveal a significantly larger total economic cost than previously estimated, and that the distribution of this economic burden across countries and regions is disproportionately influenced by local healthcare capacity and demographic structure.
proposals.method: We will first generate updated, subnational estimates of AMR mortality and years of life lost (YLLs) for 1990–2024 using GBD 2023 methods, incorporating microbial species and drug class data. These health burden estimates will be integrated into a human capital-augmented macroeconomic model, specifically a multi-country, multi-sector Solow growth model adapted with parameters for health shock persistence. The integration will use a cause-deleted life table approach to model counterfactual population structures and labor productivity, linking specific AMR-attributable deaths and YLLs to economic output via age-specific and education-adjusted wage rates. Analysis will be conducted at the GBD super-region, region, and national levels.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first comprehensive, spatially explicit estimates of the macroeconomic burden of AMR, expressed in terms of GDP loss and forgone economic growth from 1990–2024. It will provide policymakers with a unified framework to compare the cost of inaction against the investment needed for antimicrobial stewardship, infection control, and drug development. The results will directly inform economic arguments within global health security agendas.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have estimated AMR's health burden or used simplistic cost-of-illness models, this work is novel in its direct integration of high-resolution GBD-style epidemiological estimates with a dynamic macroeconomic growth model to quantify long-term, systemic economic impacts. The subnational granularity of the health data within a global economic framework is also a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Subnational estimation of the burden of antimicrobial resistance: a Bayesian small-area modelling analysis for China, India, and the United States, 1990–2024
proposals.motivation: The global burden of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a critical public health threat, but policy responses require granular data. Existing Global Burden of Disease (GBD) estimates for AMR are primarily national, masking substantial within-country heterogeneity in healthcare access, antibiotic use, and pathogen prevalence. Producing reliable subnational estimates for large, populous countries is essential for targeting interventions and resource allocation effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating national AMR burden estimates with subnational covariates (e.g., healthcare access, sanitation, antibiotic consumption) and cause-specific mortality data, we can generate robust, spatially smoothed estimates of AMR deaths and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) at the first administrative division level (states/provinces).
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 AMR modelling framework using a Bayesian hierarchical small-area estimation model. The model will downscale national AMR death rates by cause, pathogen, and drug class using spatially varying covariates via integrated nested Laplace approximation (INLA). Subnational all-cause and cause-specific mortality data from GBD subnational studies, along with geospatial priors for smoothing, will constrain estimates. Model validation will employ out-of-sample predictive checks using held-back subnational data points.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, internally consistent set of subnational AMR burden estimates for three of the world's most populous countries, directly informing their national action plans. It will provide a replicable methodological framework for subnational burden disaggregation applicable to other causes within the GBD study.
proposals.novelty: While GBD has produced subnational estimates for diseases like cancer and malaria, applying the small-area estimation paradigm to the complex, multi-pathogen, multi-drug class problem of AMR burden at the subnational level is a novel and methodologically challenging advancement.

proposals.title: Forecasting the global and national burden of cancer to 2050 under alternative policy intervention scenarios: a comparative modelling analysis
proposals.motivation: Current burden forecasts, such as those from the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) Study 2023, project future cancer prevalence, incidence, and mortality based on reference trends. However, these projections do not quantify the potential health gains from specific, scalable policy interventions. Policymakers urgently need evidence-based, comparative scenario analyses to inform priority-setting and investment in cancer control strategies globally and in key countries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modelling discrete, evidence-based policy scenarios—such as tobacco control acceleration, HPV vaccination scale-up, and targeted screening expansion—will reveal substantially different and more favorable future cancer burden trajectories compared to the reference forecast, with significant variation in impact across world regions and country income levels.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 cancer forecasting framework by integrating a comparative risk assessment (CRA) approach. For each intervention scenario, we will define a time-varying exposure distribution (e.g., smoking prevalence, vaccination coverage) based on pre-2026 efficacy data and feasible rollout targets. Using DisMod-MR 2.1 and Cause of Death Ensemble modeling (CODEm) architectures, we will propagate changes in exposure through established etiological effect sizes to generate alternative forecasts of cancer-specific mortality, incidence, and prevalence from 2024 to 2050 for 204 countries and territories.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic, global set of counterfactual cancer burden forecasts tied to concrete policy actions. It will provide a quantitative tool for assessing the relative impact and timing of different interventions, directly informing the strategic planning of national health ministries and international agencies like the WHO. The results will shift the discourse from passive prediction to active policy evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While GBD forecasts reference trends and past CRA quantifies historical attributable burden, this study innovatively combines these methodologies to project the *future* attributable burden of hypothetical policy shifts, creating a forward-looking policy evaluation tool grounded in the comprehensive GBD analytical ecosystem.

proposals.title: Harmonizing Global Gout Prevalence: A Meta-Analytic Framework for Bias Adjustment of Heterogeneous Diagnostic Criteria
proposals.motivation: Global Burden of Disease (GBD) estimates for gout are hampered by substantial heterogeneity in case definitions across studies and countries, ranging from clinical ACR criteria to administrative codes and hyperuricemia thresholds. This methodological variation obscures true epidemiological patterns, confounds cross-national comparisons of disease burden, and undermines the accuracy of global and subnational estimates. A robust framework to correct for these diagnostic biases is essential for producing comparable prevalence data to inform health policy and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: A statistical framework that models the sensitivity and specificity of different gout case definitions can successfully adjust observed prevalence estimates, allowing for the generation of harmonized, bias-corrected global and regional prevalence estimates where residual variation reflects true epidemiological differences rather than methodological artifacts.
proposals.method: We will extend Bayesian meta-regression tools used in GBD to incorporate study-level diagnostic criteria as covariates that modify study sensitivity and specificity. For each study, we will define a latent 'true prevalence' and model the observed prevalence as a function of this latent value and the study's diagnostic 'detection threshold', parameterized using prior distributions from validation studies. The core algorithm will be a hierarchical model implemented in Stan, performing probabilistic bias adjustment to output posterior distributions of harmonized prevalence for all location-years, explicitly partitioning variance components.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first set of globally comparable, bias-adjusted prevalence estimates for gout, directly quantifying the contribution of diagnostic heterogeneity to observed variation. It will provide a methodological template applicable to other diseases with heterogeneous case definitions (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis, COPD) within the GBD study, enhancing the comparability of non-fatal burden estimates.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovatively adapts completeness-correction methods from astrophysics and infectious disease modeling (e.g., for malaria incidence under varying detection) to the challenge of non-communicable disease epidemiology, moving beyond simple meta-regression to explicitly model the diagnostic process as a source of measurement error.

proposals.title: A dynamic downscaling framework for projecting the demographic structure of cancer burden: integrating country-level development covariates into the Global Burden of Disease Study
proposals.motivation: Current Global Burden of Disease (GBD) forecasts for cancer and other non-communicable diseases often rely on fixed or slowly evolving age-sex patterns, assuming historical relationships hold constant. This is a critical limitation, as epidemiological transitions driven by economic development, behavioral changes, and healthcare access can fundamentally alter disease demographics, such as the male-to-female incidence ratio or the age of peak prevalence. A method to dynamically model these shifts is essential for producing accurate, scenario-based health forecasts to inform long-term policy.
proposals.hypothesis: That country-level developmental covariates (e.g., Socio-demographic Index, healthcare access, smoking prevalence) can be used to statistically downscale and dynamically modulate core disease parameters, enabling the demographic pattern of disease burden to shift non-stationarily in future projections.
proposals.method: We will adapt statistical downscaling techniques from climate science, specifically bias-correction and spatial disaggregation methods. For key cancers (e.g., lung, breast, colorectal), we will model core parameters (age-standardized incidence rate, age-sex prevalence ratio) as functions of time-varying covariates using mixed-effects or Bayesian hierarchical models within the GBD analytical framework. The framework will be trained on historical GBD 1990-2023 data and validated using out-of-sample predictive performance. Future projections under Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) will then dynamically adjust these parameters based on projected covariate trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable modeling framework for the GBD study that moves beyond static demographic patterns, directly linking epidemiological shifts to development pathways. It will yield more realistic, scenario-dependent forecasts of cancer burden by age and sex at the national level, enhancing the utility of GBD projections for strategic health planning. The methodology can be extended to other disease categories in future GBD cycles.
proposals.novelty: The explicit cross-disciplinary analogy and technical adaptation of climate downscaling methods to solve a core problem in epidemiological forecasting—the non-stationarity of demographic patterns—is novel. It moves beyond simple covariate adjustment to a formal framework for dynamically reshaping disease parameter distributions based on future scenarios.

proposals.title: Projecting the global cancer burden to 2050: a counterfactual scenario analysis of the impact of rising obesity and dietary risk on age-standardized incidence
proposals.motivation: Current long-term forecasts of the global cancer burden, such as those from GBD 2023, primarily attribute future case growth to population aging and growth. However, the global prevalence of obesity and poor diet is rising rapidly, acting as key risk factors for several major cancers. A critical gap exists in quantifying a scenario where these metabolic and dietary risks become the primary driver of future cancer incidence, not just demographic change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under a scenario of continued global increases in high body-mass index (BMI) and dietary risks, the age-standardized incidence rates for obesity-related cancers (e.g., colorectal, pancreatic, postmenopausal breast) will rise significantly, inverting the typical forecast narrative and leading to a substantially higher total cancer burden by 2050 than predicted by demographic models alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 comparative risk assessment framework. Using spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression, we will project age-sex-location-specific exposure levels for high BMI and key dietary risks (e.g., low fruit intake, high red meat consumption) to 2050, based on pre-2026 trends from 1990-2023. We will then apply cancer-specific risk-deleted incidence rates and population attributable fractions to generate counterfactual forecasts. This will be compared to a baseline demographic-only forecast using the same DisMod-MR 3.0 architecture for cancer modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first global, comparative quantification of a high-risk metabolic scenario for future cancer burden, moving beyond passive demographic projections. It will identify which countries and cancer types are most vulnerable to shifts in these risk factors, offering a powerful evidence base for prioritizing preventive public health interventions over mere health system scaling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work forecasts cancer burden, it treats risk factor trends as relatively stable background inputs. This proposal explicitly models a scenario where adverse risk trajectories become the dominant engine of case growth, challenging the assumption that future cancer control is primarily a demographic and treatment-access problem.

proposals.title: Mapping the global burden of gout across a multidimensional socioeconomic niche: an ecological niche modelling approach using Global Burden of Disease data, 1990–2023
proposals.motivation: Gout, a painful inflammatory arthritis, imposes a significant and growing global Years Lived with Disability (YLD) burden. Current Global Burden of Disease (GBD) analyses often stratify results by geographic region or broad Socio-demographic Index (SDI) quintiles, which may obscure the complex, non-linear interactions between specific socioeconomic, dietary, and healthcare system factors that drive gout risk. A more etiologically informative, multidimensional model is needed to guide targeted national and subnational policy interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: National-level estimates of gout prevalence and YLDs can be accurately modeled as a function of a continuous, multidimensional socioeconomic 'niche', defined by variables including HDI components, income inequality, healthcare access, urbanization, and dietary patterns, with key drivers exhibiting non-linear thresholds analogous to species' ecological limits.
proposals.method: We will apply Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) techniques, specifically MaxEnt (Maximum Entropy) and/or Generalized Additive Models (GAMs), to GBD 2023 gout estimates for 204 countries and territories from 1990–2023. The 'species occurrence' data will be national gout YLD rates, and the 'environmental layers' will be a curated set of spatial-temporal covariates from pre-2026 sources (e.g., World Bank, FAO, WHO). Model performance will be evaluated via spatial cross-validation, and variable importance will be assessed to identify key socioeconomic and dietary drivers.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first global, continuous-risk map of gout burden based on its socioeconomic niche, moving beyond categorical SDI groupings. It will identify the most salient, potentially modifiable drivers (e.g., specific dietary factors at different development levels) and provide a predictive framework to forecast gout burden under different development scenarios, directly informing the GBD's comparative risk assessment framework.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly adapting core ENM algorithms from ecology to model a non-communicable disease burden as a function of a multivariate socioeconomic environment, creating a continuous, etiologically grounded alternative to standard categorical development indices used in global health.

proposals.title: A life-course epidemiology analysis of sex disparities in gout: a multi-cohort study to model risk factor trajectories and critical periods, 1990–2025
proposals.motivation: Gout exhibits one of the most pronounced sex disparities in non-communicable disease, with a global male-to-female prevalence ratio of approximately 3:1, yet the drivers of this disparity across the lifespan remain poorly quantified. Understanding this is critical for equitable prevention, as existing burden estimates from the Global Burden of Disease Study and others treat sex as a simple stratification, not a dynamic modifier of risk pathways over time.
proposals.hypothesis: The male predominance in gout is not solely due to static biological differences but results from the differential accumulation and interaction of modifiable risk factors (e.g., diet, renal function, metabolic syndrome) with sex-specific hormonal milieus across critical life stages, particularly the menopausal transition in women.
proposals.method: We will harmonize individual-level longitudinal data from major cohorts (e.g., Framingham, ARIC, UK Biobank, and relevant Asian cohorts) to construct sex-specific trajectories of serum urate, eGFR, BMI, dietary patterns, and hormone levels from early adulthood. Using pooled survival analysis and multi-state modeling, we will estimate age-specific hazard ratios for gout by sex, adjusting for time-varying exposures. We will apply g-computation to simulate counterfactual risk under altered life-course exposure patterns to quantify the population-attributable fraction of the sex disparity.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first formal life-course model of gout risk, identifying critical windows (e.g., perimenopause) and cumulative exposure thresholds that explain sex differences. The findings will directly inform sex-stratified prevention strategies in global burden of disease frameworks and provide a methodological template for analyzing sex disparities in other complex conditions.
proposals.novelty: While life-course approaches are established for cardiovascular disease, their application to gout is novel. This moves beyond cross-sectional prevalence comparisons to dynamically model how risk unfolds differently over the lifespan for men and women, integrating biological and behavioral data on a scale not previously attempted for this condition.

proposals.title: Mapping subnational gout prevalence risk using an ecological species distribution modeling framework: a geospatial modelling study for 2000–2024
proposals.motivation: Gout, a painful and debilitating form of inflammatory arthritis, imposes a significant and growing global burden, yet its subnational distribution remains poorly characterized due to sparse and inconsistent health data. This data gap hinders efficient resource allocation and targeted public health interventions. High-resolution, small-area estimates are critical for health systems planning, particularly in low- and middle-income countries where the epidemiological transition is altering disease profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial distribution of gout prevalence risk can be accurately modeled at subnational scales by adapting Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) algorithms, using sparse clinical or survey case data as occurrence points and gout-relevant sociodemographic, dietary, and metabolic covariates as environmental predictors.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MaxEnt (Maximum Entropy) algorithm, a widely used presence-background SDM, to model gout prevalence risk. Sparse gout 'occurrence' data will be sourced from nationally representative surveys (e.g., WHO STEPS, Demographic and Health Surveys) and the GBD 2023 study's subnational data. High-resolution geospatial covariates (e.g., night-time lights for urbanization, satellite-derived food environment proxies, subnational obesity estimates from NCD-RisC, and poverty indices) will serve as predictors. Model performance will be validated via cross-validation and, where possible, against held-out subnational data.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first high-resolution, probabilistic maps of gout prevalence risk for subnational regions globally, identifying previously unrecognized high-burden areas. It will provide a novel methodological framework for estimating the burden of other data-sparse non-communicable diseases by transferring robust ecological modeling techniques to population health.
proposals.novelty: The application of Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) from ecology to model a non-communicable disease like gout is a novel methodological crossover. While spatial modeling is used in infectious disease epidemiology (e.g., malaria mapping), its structured application to a chronic condition driven by metabolic and lifestyle factors, using analogous 'environmental' layers, is non-obvious and represents a significant innovation in small-area estimation.

proposals.title: A geospatial microsimulation framework for forecasting subnational gout prevalence and healthcare resource gaps to 2050: an analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study
proposals.motivation: The global burden of gout is rising, driven by aging populations and dietary shifts, yet healthcare planning remains reactive and coarse. Existing Global Burden of Disease (GBD) forecasts provide national or large subnational estimates, which mask critical local disparities in disease risk and healthcare infrastructure. Proactive, equitable resource allocation requires high-resolution, spatially explicit models that link future disease burden with current healthcare capacity to identify specific regions at risk of being overwhelmed.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating small-area estimation techniques with healthcare capacity data can generate high-resolution, subnational forecasts of gout prevalence and simulate future care gaps, revealing specific geographic inequities not apparent in current national-level models.
proposals.method: We will extend GBD 2023 gout forecasts to 2050 using a Bayesian geostatistical microsimulation framework. Spatially explicit covariates (e.g., subnational aging trends from GBD, dietary risk factor data, urbanicity) will be incorporated via a modified Fay-Herriot model for small-area estimation. This model will downscale projected burden to a 5x5 km resolution. We will then fuse these prevalence estimates with geolocated datasets on current rheumatology workforce density and healthcare facility capacity using a spatial linkage algorithm.
