A discrete-event simulation module will model patient flow against capacity constraints to quantify future care gaps.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, global map of forecasted gout prevalence and associated healthcare resource gaps, directly actionable for health system planners. It will establish a reproducible microsimulation framework that can be applied to other non-communicable diseases within the GBD paradigm, shifting health policy from reactive to pre-emptive planning.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion of poverty-mapping small-area estimation techniques with healthcare capacity forecasting for a non-communicable disease represents a significant methodological advance beyond standard GBD reporting. It moves from describing burden to simulating its interaction with health systems at a granular, policy-relevant scale.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Data-Assimilative Framework for Estimating the Population-Level Burden of Co-Occurring Causes of Death with Explicit Uncertainty Quantification: A Global Burden of Disease Sub-Study
proposals.motivation: In real-world mortality data, especially from low-resource settings or for older populations, deaths are often attributed to a single underlying cause despite frequent multimorbidity and interacting disease pathways. This leads to systematic misclassification in cause-of-death statistics, biasing estimates of disease burden and obscuring the true impact of co-occurring conditions like cancer, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes. Existing Global Burden of Disease (GBD) models, while robust, typically rely on redistribution algorithms and counterfactual single-cause frameworks that may not fully capture the joint mortality risk and uncertainty arising from imperfect, fragmented data.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian hierarchical model that integrates multi-resolution, noisy mortality data with probabilistic constraints based on known pathophysiological interactions can produce valid, uncertainty-quantified estimates of the population-level burden attributable to co-occurring causes, even in the absence of perfect, granular cause-of-death records.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian data-assimilative framework using integrated nested Laplace approximations (INLA) for computational efficiency. The model will integrate individual-level verbal autopsy data, aggregated vital registration statistics, and population-based cohort study findings as multi-likelihood components. Probabilistic constraints will be encoded via informed prior distributions derived from meta-analyses of clinical studies on disease interactions (e.g., how diabetes modifies cancer mortality risk). A key innovation is an explicit misclassification model within the hierarchy, treating the true, unobserved cause-of-death combination as a latent variable informed by the fragmented data inputs and the interaction priors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for the GBD study and similar efforts to move beyond the independent-cause paradigm, producing more realistic estimates of co-morbid mortality burden. It will deliver a publicly available tool for quantifying the uncertainty introduced by cause-of-death misclassification, directly informing the interpretation of mortality statistics used for priority setting. The approach will be validated in a case study on cancer and cardiometabolic co-mortality across countries with varying data quality.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous burden models that correct for data gaps via redistribution or assume independence, this framework explicitly models the joint probability of co-occurring causes as a latent state, directly assimilating fragmented data sources while quantifying uncertainty from the ground up. It formalizes the integration of pathophysiological knowledge as probabilistic constraints within a statistical estimation problem, rather than as a post-hoc adjustment.

proposals.title: A Global Burden of Disease Subnational Analysis of Protective Multi-Morbidity Interactions and Exceptional Survival Pathways, 1990-2023
proposals.motivation: Global Burden of Disease (GBD) studies have meticulously quantified how diseases compete to cause death, framing multi-morbidity as a key driver of mortality risk. However, this paradigm may overlook populations where the co-occurrence of conditions is associated with better-than-expected survival, potentially revealing latent systemic resilience. Identifying these 'exceptions' is crucial for shifting public health strategy from siloed prevention to cultivating synergistic health supports that extend life even in the presence of multiple conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, measurable interactions between prevalent diseases (e.g., certain cancers and chronic infections) or between diseases and systemic factors (e.g., healthcare access, socioeconomic status) function as dynamic modifiers that attenuate mortality risk, creating identifiable pathways to exceptional survival at subnational levels globally.
proposals.method: We will extend GBD 2023 comparative risk assessment and cause-of-death ensemble modeling frameworks. Using DisMod-MR 3.0 and multi-state Markov models, we will treat disease pairs not just as competing risks but as interacting states, estimating hazard ratios for mortality conditional on joint prevalence. We will employ spatial-temporal Bayesian models to identify subnational regions (e.g., Japanese prefectures, US counties) where observed life expectancy or cause-deleted LE significantly exceeds model predictions based on individual disease burdens, then use machine learning (gradient boosting) on GBD covariate data to identify resilience-associated factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global, subnational map of 'resilience outliers' where multi-morbidity correlates with extended survival. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for modeling cooperative, rather than strictly competing, health risks within the GBD architecture. The findings will provide empirical targets for policies aimed at building systemic health buffers.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the foundational competing risks model of population health to systematically search for and explain protective disease interactions, a perspective absent from prior GBD analyses focused on additive and attributable risks. The integration of multi-state models for synergy detection within a global comparative framework is methodologically unprecedented.

proposals.title: A Bayesian competing-risks framework for modelling mortality from interacting causes: a global burden of disease study
proposals.motivation: Standard Global Burden of Disease (GBD) analyses treat causes of death as mutually exclusive, an assumption increasingly violated in aging populations with multimorbidity. The COVID-19 pandemic starkly revealed this limitation, as mortality risk was heavily modified by co-existing conditions like cardiovascular disease and diabetes. Quantifying the joint and attributable mortality from interacting causes is critical for accurate burden estimation and effective health policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a competing-risks model accounting for cause interaction and correlation will produce materially different estimates of cause-specific mortality fractions and years of life lost (YLLs) compared to the standard independent-categories approach, particularly for causes with shared risk factors or synergistic pathophysiology.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD cause-of-death ensemble model (CODEm) framework by implementing a Bayesian multinomial logistic regression model with a structured covariance prior over cause-specific probabilities. This will model dependence between causes (e.g., COVID-19 and chronic kidney disease) using country-level random effects and individual-level risk factor data. Model fitting will use Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via Stan, validated against detailed vital registration data from high-quality sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first internally consistent, global estimates of mortality attributable to co-occurring causes, moving beyond simplistic partitioning. It will deliver a new methodological standard for burden estimation in multimorbid populations and offer policymakers a clearer picture of the joint mortality burden from interacting conditions.
proposals.novelty: While competing-risks models exist in biostatistics, their integration into the large-scale, hierarchical, spatiotemporal modeling architecture of the GBD is novel. This moves from statistical correction of garbage codes to a fundamental reconceptualization of cause-of-death assignment in population health metrics.

proposals.title: Causal Regularization for Non-Stationary Age-of-Death Distributions in Global Burden of Disease: A Competing Risks Framework
proposals.motivation: Accurate modeling of shifting age patterns of mortality is critical for forecasting disease burden and evaluating health interventions. Traditional methods in the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) studies often assume stable age patterns or struggle to isolate the direct effect of interventions from time-varying confounding due to competing risks and cohort-specific exposures, leading to biased forecasts and policy assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating causal directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) with penalized functional regression will yield more robust, generalizable estimates of non-stationary cause-specific age-at-death distributions by explicitly adjusting for competing mortality risks and protecting against overfitting to sparse subnational data.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD cause-of-death ensemble model (CODEm) framework. First, we will specify causal DAGs to identify adjustment sets for isolating direct effects on age patterns. Second, we will implement a penalized functional regression model, treating the age-specific mortality curve as a smooth function of time and cohort exposures, using P-splines with roughness penalties. Third, model selection and hyperparameter tuning will be performed via cross-validation on out-of-sample predictive performance across geographies and time periods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, causally-informed estimation tool for the GBD study, improving the accuracy of forecasts for cancer and other diseases. It will deliver a publicly available framework for decomposing temporal shifts in mortality age patterns into components attributable to specific interventions versus changes in competing risks.
proposals.novelty: The integration of formal causal identification strategies with functional data regularization for mortality age patterns is a novel synthesis beyond current GBD methods. It moves from associative smoothing to a model that explicitly targets estimands for intervention effects in a competing risks setting.

proposals.title: Dynamic Age-Niche Shifts in Cause-Specific Mortality: A Global Analysis of Cancer Epidemiological Transition, 1990–2023
proposals.motivation: Current models for the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) often assume fixed or limitedly varying age patterns for cause-specific mortality, potentially obscuring key epidemiological dynamics. For major causes like cancer, secular trends in risk exposure, diagnosis, and treatment likely alter not just the total mortality rate but the fundamental age distribution of deaths. Quantifying these shifts is critical for accurate forecasting and for evaluating whether public health gains are compressing or delaying mortality across the lifespan.
proposals.hypothesis: The age profile of mortality for specific cancers (e.g., lung, breast, colorectal) functions as a dynamic 'niche' whose central tendency, dispersion, and shape have systematically shifted over the past three decades, reflecting changes in risk factors, screening, and therapy, and these shifts vary significantly across countries and development levels.
proposals.method: We will adapt ecological niche modeling techniques, specifically time-parameterized versions of Gaussian or skewed probability density functions, to represent the age-at-death distribution for each cause, country, sex, and year. Using GBD 2023 cause-of-death data, we will estimate time-series of parameters (e.g., modal age, variance, skewness) via Bayesian penalized spline regression or a hierarchical modeling framework. This will allow us to formally test for temporal trends in these parameters, classifying shifts as delays (increasing mode), compressions (decreasing variance), or shape changes, and correlate them with country-level covariates like the Socio-demographic Index and healthcare access.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first formal, global quantification of temporal shifts in the age patterns of cancer mortality, moving beyond summary rates. It will produce a novel set of metrics (e.g., 'age-shift velocity') for epidemiological surveillance and significantly improve the realism of cause-specific mortality forecasts in the GBD framework by incorporating dynamic age patterns.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual and methodological borrowing from spatial ecology to model demographic distributions, treating age as an analog to an ecological gradient, and in its systematic application to decompose cause-specific mortality change into changes in scale versus changes in age structure.

proposals.title: Modelling the non-stationary age patterns of cause-specific mortality: a flexible framework for the Global Burden of Disease Study
proposals.motivation: Current Global Burden of Disease (GBD) models often assume stable age patterns of mortality for specific causes over time, which may not reflect reality. Major public health interventions (e.g., screening, new therapies), cohort-specific risk exposures, and shifting incidence can alter the age distribution of deaths, leading to systematic errors in mortality estimation and forecasting. A robust method to capture these shifts is critical for accurate burden assessment and health policy planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling cause-specific mortality with flexible, non-stationary age patterns will significantly improve the accuracy of mortality estimates and forecasts compared to models with fixed age patterns, particularly for causes like specific cancers where prevention and treatment have evolved rapidly.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing GBD cause of death ensemble modeling (CODEm) framework. Instead of using fixed age weights, we will incorporate time-varying splines or Gaussian process priors on age-specific mortality rates within the ensemble's constituent models (e.g., spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression, mixed-effects models). This will allow the age pattern to smoothly evolve as a function of time and development covariates (e.g., Socio-demographic Index). Model performance will be validated using out-of-sample predictive accuracy on historical data from 1990-2023.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the GBD study with a validated, generalizable method for modeling dynamic mortality age patterns, improving the accuracy of historical estimates and future forecasts. It will yield new insights into how the age burden of major diseases like cancer has shifted globally and subnationally over the past three decades.
proposals.novelty: While prior GBD work has modeled temporal trends in rates, explicitly modeling the *shape* of the age curve as a non-stationary process within the established ensemble framework is a novel integration. This moves beyond simple age-period-cohort models by embedding the flexibility directly into the core estimation machinery used for global comparative assessments.

proposals.title: Augmenting Global Burden of Disease Estimation: A Bayesian Spatiotemporal Framework Integrating Digital Health Records for Cause-Specific Mortality in Data-Sparse Regions
proposals.motivation: Accurate cause-of-death estimation is critical for global health priority-setting but remains severely constrained by the scarcity and low quality of vital registration data in many regions. Pre-2026, the expansion of digital health systems, including hospital electronic health records (EHRs) and community health information platforms, offers a novel, high-frequency data stream that is largely untapped for national and subnational mortality modeling. Integrating these sources could reduce uncertainty in burden estimates for key causes like cancers and infectious diseases where local data are absent.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically integrating structured data from digital health records into established Bayesian Cause of Death Ensemble modeling (CODEm) frameworks will significantly improve the accuracy and precision of cause-specific mortality estimates and their trends in countries with incomplete vital registration systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular extension to the GBD's CODEm architecture. The method involves: 1) Creating a standardized pipeline for extracting, geolocating, and harmonizing cause-of-death indicators from de-identified, aggregate-level EHR datasets from partner networks in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. 2) Employing a two-stage Bayesian spatiotemporal modeling approach where digital record-derived mortality fractions are treated as an additional, spatially explicit data layer with quantified measurement error. 3) Using integrated nested Laplace approximations (INLA) to fit models that jointly estimate cause-specific mortality rates from traditional (e.g., verbal autopsy) and novel digital data streams, leveraging their spatial and temporal correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source methodological framework for incorporating non-traditional digital data into global health metrics. It will generate updated, more granular estimates of cause-specific mortality for 1990-2025 in selected data-sparse countries, directly informing the GBD study. The project will also establish best-practice guidelines for data partnerships and bias adjustment for digital health records in population health research.
proposals.novelty: While previous GBD work has integrated diverse data types, the systematic, model-based fusion of high-frequency, subnational digital health records with traditional population-level data sources for core mortality estimation represents a novel advance. The methodological innovation lies in formally quantifying and propagating the unique error structures of digital records within an ensemble modeling environment.

proposals.title: A Bayesian small-area estimation framework for 1 km² resolution mortality and burden of disease mapping: a validation and application study for cancer in Japan, 1990–2021
proposals.motivation: Current subnational burden of disease analyses, such as the GBD study's 811 locations, often mask critical intra-regional heterogeneity, limiting the precision of public health resource allocation and intervention targeting. Higher spatial resolution estimates are essential for identifying localized disease hotspots and health inequities, particularly for conditions like cancer where environmental and socioeconomic risk factors vary sharply over small geographic scales. This need aligns with the growing demand for granular, data-driven health policy, as evidenced by subnational analyses in countries like Japan.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating high-resolution gridded data on population, covariates, and environmental exposures with a Bayesian hierarchical modeling framework can produce valid and reliable 1 km² resolution estimates of cancer mortality, significantly reducing estimation uncertainty in data-sparse areas compared to existing administrative-level models.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD's Cause of Death Ensemble model (CODEm) and spatial-temporal Gaussian process regression into a Bayesian small-area estimation framework. This framework will integrate 1 km² gridded population data (WorldPop), satellite-derived covariates (e.g., night-time lights, land use), and high-resolution pollution and socioeconomic surfaces. We will employ integrated nested Laplace approximations (INLA) for computationally efficient inference, using a modified Besag-York-Mollié model to incorporate spatial autocorrelation and borrow strength across adjacent grid cells. Model validation will be performed via cross-validation against held-out vital registration data in Japan.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-access, global 1 km² resolution maps of cancer mortality for key sites, setting a new standard for spatial granularity in burden estimation. It will provide a validated, generalizable methodological pipeline for high-resolution disease mapping that can be applied to other GBD causes. The results will directly enable micro-targeting of screening programs and healthcare infrastructure in high-burden clusters previously invisible at coarser resolutions.
proposals.novelty: While spatial modeling in GBD is established, moving to a continuous, fine-scale gridded framework at 1 km² represents a significant leap beyond fixed administrative units. The novel integration of a vast array of high-resolution covariate layers within a computationally tractable Bayesian INLA architecture for global burden estimation is untested at this scale.

proposals.title: Mapping the Socioeconomic Gradient of Cause-Specific Mortality: A High-Resolution Geospatial Analysis of Health Equity for the Global Burden of Disease Study
proposals.motivation: Despite global progress in reducing all-cause mortality, profound health inequities persist within countries, often masked by national averages. Identifying the precise geographic and socioeconomic strata bearing the highest burden of specific diseases is critical for equitable health policy and resource allocation, a core challenge in population health. Pre-2026, while the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study provides subnational estimates, systematic, high-resolution mapping of mortality by cause against multidimensional socioeconomic deprivation remains underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating small-area socioeconomic deprivation indices with cause-specific mortality estimates will reveal distinct, persistent geographic hotspots of inequity, where the burden from amenable causes (e.g., ischemic heart disease, stroke, road injuries, lung cancer) is disproportionately concentrated in the most deprived communities, even within high-income countries.
proposals.method: We will adapt the cumulative burden mapping framework from environmental justice. Using GBD 2023 cause-of-death estimates at the most granular subnational level available, we will construct a multidimensional area deprivation index (ADI) for each location from census and survey data (e.g., income, education, occupation, housing). We will employ Bayesian spatial-temporal models, akin to those used in GBD malaria mapping, to smooth estimates and quantify uncertainty. These layers will be integrated in a geographic information system (GIS) to calculate location-specific mortality-to-deprivation ratios, identifying statistically significant hotspots via spatial scan statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global, subnational atlas of cause-specific mortality inequity, quantifying the mortality burden attributable to socioeconomic deprivation. It will provide policymakers with a precise, visual tool to target interventions to the communities and specific disease drivers where they are most needed to reduce disparities, directly operationalizing the 'precision public health' paradigm for health equity.
proposals.novelty: While environmental justice mapping of pollution is established, its systematic application to decompose the global burden of disease by cause and socioeconomic stratum at high resolution is novel. This moves beyond describing disparities to explicitly mapping the joint distribution of mortality risk and social disadvantage, creating a diagnostic tool for health equity analogous to pollution exposure maps.

proposals.title: Projecting Achievable Cancer Mortality Reduction to 2040: An Agent-Based Simulation Integrating Global Burden of Disease Estimates and Real-World Intervention Scalability
proposals.motivation: Current Global Burden of Disease (GBD) estimates quantify the theoretical burden attributable to risk factors but do not model how this burden can be feasibly reduced given real-world constraints on health systems, resources, and intervention delivery. Policymakers require projections of 'achievable burden reduction' under specific, scalable intervention portfolios to set realistic targets and allocate resources efficiently, particularly for complex diseases like cancer where interventions interact.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding GBD 2023 estimates of cancer incidence, prevalence, and mortality within a dynamic, stochastic simulation of a population will demonstrate that the achievable mortality reduction from 2025 to 2040 is substantially lower than the theoretical risk-attributable burden, due to delivery constraints, costs, and non-linear interactions between prevention, screening, and treatment interventions.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) where synthetic agents, parameterized with GBD 2023 demographic, risk, and cancer-type data, transition through health states based on discrete-time stochastic processes. Intervention portfolios (e.g., tobacco control, HPV vaccination, screening expansion, treatment access) will be implemented as time-varying modifiers of transition probabilities, with coverage and efficacy constrained by country-specific cost and health system capacity data from WHO and World Bank. We will use a Bayesian calibration approach to fit the model to GBD historical trends and then run Monte Carlo simulations to project mortality under different policy scenarios, explicitly modeling synergistic and antagonistic effects between interventions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, directly actionable tool for health policy, shifting the output from static attribution to dynamic, scenario-based projections of lives saved. It will provide quantified estimates of the gap between theoretical and achievable cancer mortality reduction for a representative set of countries, highlighting critical system bottlenecks. The methodology will establish a framework for extending this approach to other disease groups within the GBD.
proposals.novelty: While GBD studies forecast disease burden, they do not simulate the implementation pathways of interventions. This proposal integrates the comprehensive GBD evidence base with an ABM architecture to explicitly model the dynamic, constrained rollout of intervention packages, moving beyond comparative risk assessment to operational feasibility.

proposals.title: Causal attribution of national life expectancy gains to major health policies: a global comparative analysis using synthetic control methods, 1990–2023
proposals.motivation: While the Global Burden of Disease Study has meticulously documented trends in mortality and life expectancy across countries, the specific contribution of discrete health policies to these changes remains largely associative. Policymakers urgently need robust, counterfactual-based evidence on which interventions—such as tobacco control, cancer screening programs, or universal health coverage expansions—most effectively extend population life. This study directly addresses that gap by applying formal causal inference to global health data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic control analyses will identify statistically significant, policy-attributable gains in life expectancy and reductions in cause-specific mortality (e.g., cancer) for nations that implemented major, well-defined health interventions compared to their synthetic counterfactuals.
proposals.method: We will leverage the GBD 2023 cause-of-death and life expectancy estimates for 204 countries and territories from 1990–2023. For selected policy 'shocks' (e.g., Japan's 2003 Health Promotion Law, Turkey's 2008 Universal Health Insurance), we will construct a synthetic control unit for the treated country using a weighted combination of donor pool countries that had similar pre-intervention trends in mortality, GDP, and healthcare access. The causal effect will be estimated as the post-intervention difference in life expectancy between the treated unit and its synthetic control. Robustness will be tested via placebo tests and difference-in-differences models on subnational GBD data where applicable.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global catalog of causally estimated life expectancy impacts for a range of key health policies, moving beyond correlation. It will provide a methodological template for integrating GBD estimates with causal inference for policy evaluation. The findings will directly inform priority-setting by identifying the policies with the highest demonstrated mortality returns.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic control is established in economics, its systematic application to global, longitudinal burden of disease data for cross-national health policy evaluation is novel. This approach moves past ecological association to provide a rigorous counterfactual framework for assessing the mortality impact of real-world policy decisions.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Vital Statistics Systems: Determining Minimal Coverage to Achieve Target Uncertainty in National Mortality Estimates
proposals.motivation: Robust mortality data are foundational for tracking global health progress, yet many countries lack complete civil registration and vital statistics (CRVS) systems. Current methods, like those used in the Global Burden of Disease Study, model mortality where data are sparse, but they do not provide a clear, quantitative framework for policymakers to invest in data systems. This creates a gap between global estimation goals and national capacity-building priorities.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given country context, there exists a quantifiable, non-linear relationship between the completeness of a vital statistics system and the precision of national mortality estimates, allowing for the derivation of minimum data coverage thresholds to meet specific uncertainty targets.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation framework using established GBD models (e.g., Cause of Death Ensemble modeling, spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression) as the 'forward model'. For a set of representative countries, we will simulate incomplete death registration by systematically degrading high-quality, complete data (e.g., from Japan subnational analysis). We will then apply Bayesian optimization and Monte Carlo sampling to solve the inverse problem: identifying the minimal combination of coverage (e.g., % of deaths registered) and spatial/demographic representativeness that keeps the 95% uncertainty interval of key mortality metrics (like age-standardized death rate) within a pre-specified bound.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first generalizable, quantitative tool for health ministries and donors to set evidence-based targets for CRVS strengthening. It will directly link data system investments to measurable improvements in estimation precision, moving beyond qualitative assessments. The framework will also identify which population subgroups are most critical to include in registration efforts to reduce overall uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: While extensive work models mortality *from* incomplete data, this proposal inverts the paradigm to design data systems *for* a desired modeling outcome. It formally frames CRVS strengthening as an optimization problem with a precision constraint, a novel application of inverse problem methodology in population health.

proposals.title: The global, regional, and national burden of cancer not attributable to known risk factors: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2025
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study has meticulously quantified the cancer burden attributable to specific risks like smoking and air pollution. However, the substantial residual burden—the portion not explained by these known risks—remains poorly characterized at a global scale. Quantifying this 'unexplained' or baseline burden is critical for setting realistic prevention targets, understanding the limits of current risk factor epidemiology, and directing research towards novel etiological factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant and geographically variable proportion of the global cancer burden, measured in deaths, disability-adjusted life years (DALYs), and incidence, is not attributable to the suite of risks currently quantified by GBD, and that this proportion will differ substantially by cancer type and Socio-demographic Index (SDI).
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing GBD 2025 comparative risk assessment (CRA) framework. For each cancer type, location, year, age, and sex, we will calculate the theoretical minimum risk exposure level (TMREL) counterfactual burden. The burden *not* attributable to known risks will be derived by subtracting the summed attributable burden for all quantified risks from the total observed burden. This will be implemented using GBD's DisMod-MR and spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression models to ensure consistency, with uncertainty propagated from all input data and risk estimates.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first comprehensive, standardized global maps of the cancer burden unexplained by current major risk factors. It will provide a crucial baseline for evaluating the potential impact of future risk factor discoveries and precision prevention strategies. The findings will inform national health priorities by highlighting the intrinsic or currently unknown cancer risk that must be addressed through early detection and treatment.
proposals.novelty: While GBD routinely reports attributable burden, this study systematically inverts that calculation to focus on the residual, offering a novel perspective on the limits of prevention. It moves beyond identifying 'what we know' to explicitly quantify 'what we do not know' in global cancer etiology.

proposals.title: Global, regional, and national burden of disease attributable to key risk factors, 1990–2024: an updated comparative risk assessment from the Global Burden of Disease Study
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic, beginning in 2020, has caused profound disruptions to health systems, economies, and lifestyles worldwide, potentially altering population exposure to major health risks like high blood pressure, smoking, and air pollution. An updated comparative risk assessment (CRA) incorporating post-2019 data is urgently needed to quantify how these disruptions have shifted risk-attributable burden, informing whether global health targets are still on track.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pandemic period (2020–2024) has led to significant reversals or accelerations in previous trends for key risk factors, resulting in measurable changes in attributable mortality and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) at global and national levels.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD CRA framework to include data through 2024. This involves systematically identifying, extracting, and synthesizing data on risk factor exposure from surveys, administrative data, and remote sensing (e.g., satellite-derived PM2.5). We will use established spatiotemporal Gaussian process regression, Cause of Death Ensemble modeling (CODEm), and DisMod-MR 3.0 to estimate exposure distributions and theoretical minimum risk exposure levels (TMRELs). Population-attributable fractions will be calculated and applied to disease burden estimates to produce final attributable burden estimates for 204 countries and territories from 1990–2024.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, standardized assessment of how risk-attributable disease burden has evolved through the pandemic era, offering a crucial evidence base for post-2020 health policy. It will update the foundational GBD risk assessment, enabling tracking of progress toward SDGs and NCD targets in light of recent global shocks.
proposals.novelty: While prior GBD CRAs end in 2019, this analysis integrates four critical post-pandemic years, formally testing for discontinuities in long-term risk trends and providing novel estimates of the pandemic's indirect effects on non-communicable disease risk exposures.

proposals.title: Expanding the Global Burden of Disease risk factor framework: a systematic integration of emerging evidence on ambient ozone, dietary fibre, and processed meat for cancer and cardiometabolic outcomes, 1990–2025
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study's 82 risk-outcome pairs, while comprehensive, require continuous updating to reflect evolving epidemiological evidence. Pre-2026 meta-analyses have strengthened causal links for ambient ozone with chronic respiratory disease and lung cancer, and for specific dietary components like low fibre and high processed meat intake with colorectal cancer and ischemic heart disease. Quantifying the attributable burden of these emerging risks is critical for timely and targeted public health policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically incorporating ambient ozone, low dietary fibre, and high processed meat consumption into the GBD comparative risk assessment framework will reveal a significant, previously unquantified proportion of the global burden from specific cancers and cardiometabolic diseases, with substantial variation across countries and over time.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD comparative risk assessment methodology. For each new risk-outcome pair (e.g., ozone–lung cancer, processed meat–colorectal cancer), we will conduct systematic reviews to define exposure distributions, theoretical minimum risk exposure levels (TMRELs), and relative risk curves, leveraging existing GBD data infrastructure. Exposure estimates will be modelled at a 0.1° × 0.1° resolution for ozone using satellite data and chemical transport models, and at the national level for dietary factors using dietary surveys and food balance sheets. Attributable deaths, DALYs, and their trends from 1990–2025 will be computed using the GBD's standard DisMod-MR 2.1 and Cause of Death Ensemble modelling (CODEm) architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first global, spatially-resolved estimates of disease burden attributable to these emerging risk factors within the standardized GBD framework. It will provide crucial evidence for the WHO and national governments to update air quality guidelines and dietary recommendations. The results will be integrated into the GBD 2025 cycle, directly influencing future priority-setting for disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While individual risks have been studied, this proposal's novelty lies in their simultaneous, methodologically consistent integration into the GBD's unified analytical engine, allowing direct comparison with established risks like PM2.5 and smoking, and revealing their interactive contribution to subnational disease patterns in countries like Japan.

proposals.title: Disaggregating the drivers of sex and age disparities in risk-attributable cancer mortality: a comparative assessment of population structure, risk exposure, and biological susceptibility, 1990–2023
proposals.motivation: Global Burden of Disease (GBD) analyses have consistently shown large sex disparities in risk-attributable cancer deaths, with men bearing a significantly higher burden. While leading risk factors like smoking and alcohol use explain part of this gap, a substantial residual disparity persists across many cancer types and age groups. A systematic, age-stratified decomposition of these disparities is needed to inform more targeted and equitable cancer control policies globally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed sex disparities in risk-attributable cancer mortality are not solely driven by differences in exposure to major behavioral and environmental risks, but are significantly mediated by interactions between age-specific population structures, sex-specific biological susceptibility, and differential access to early detection and care.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 comparative risk assessment framework. Using GBD 2023 estimates of cancer deaths, risk-attributable fractions, and risk exposure levels by sex, age, country, and year, we will perform a three-component decomposition analysis for key cancer sites. This will quantify the proportion of the sex disparity explained by: 1) differences in population age structure, 2) differences in risk exposure levels, and 3) a residual component encompassing biological susceptibility and care. We will employ a regression-based decomposition method (adapted from demographic Kitagawa decomposition) and conduct the analysis at global, regional, and national levels from 1990 to 2023.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative breakdown of the drivers of sex-age disparities in attributable cancer mortality across time and geography. It will move beyond descriptive reporting of disparities to identify which component (demography, exposure, or residual) is most influential for specific cancers and age groups. The findings will help policymakers prioritize interventions, whether they need to target risk factor reduction, improve early detection for specific sex-age groups, or fund research into biological mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While prior GBD work has quantified sex disparities in cancer burden, this proposal innovates by applying a formal demographic decomposition to risk-attributable mortality, isolating the modifiable exposure component from structural demographic and residual factors. This moves from documenting 'what' the disparity is to explaining 'why' it exists in a quantifiable way.

proposals.title: Subnational integration of exposure and cancer registry data to refine uncertainty in global cancer burden estimates: a case study analysis for 2010–2019
proposals.motivation: Global Burden of Disease (GBD) cancer estimates rely heavily on national-level data and statistical modeling, which can mask significant subnational heterogeneity in risk factor exposure and cancer outcomes. This aggregation contributes to wide uncertainty intervals, limiting the precision needed for targeted national and subnational cancer control planning. Integrating higher-resolution local data is a critical, unmet step towards more actionable and reliable burden assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically integrating subnational data on key cancer risk exposures (e.g., smoking, alcohol, air pollution) and cancer incidence from high-quality registries will significantly reduce the width of uncertainty intervals for national and subnational cancer death and disability-adjusted life year (DALY) estimates compared to current GBD models.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing GBD comparative risk assessment framework by developing a hierarchical Bayesian meta-regression model. This model will explicitly incorporate spatially resolved, subnational data on exposure distributions from sources like geospatial models and surveys, and link them to subnational cancer incidence data from registries meeting specific quality criteria (e.g., in Japan, the US, and the UK). The model will use integrated nested Laplace approximations (INLA) for spatial smoothing and uncertainty propagation, quantifying the reduction in posterior credible interval width attributable to the local data integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated methodological framework for integrating subnational data into global burden estimation, directly improving the precision of cancer burden estimates for policy-relevant subpopulations. It will produce a proof-of-concept analysis demonstrating the potential reduction in uncertainty for selected countries and cancers, offering a roadmap for future GBD cycles to systematically incorporate local data.
proposals.novelty: While GBD studies have incorporated some subnational data, a formal, quantitative assessment of how high-resolution exposure and outcome data jointly constrain estimation uncertainty at the national and subnational level represents a novel methodological advance. This moves beyond descriptive subnational reporting to a causal inference framework for precision in burden estimation.

proposals.title: The global economic cost of cancer treatment attributable to modifiable risk factors: a comparative risk assessment and cost-of-illness analysis, 1990–2023
proposals.motivation: The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) framework has meticulously quantified the years of life lost and disability from cancers due to modifiable risks like smoking and air pollution. However, the corresponding economic burden on healthcare systems—the direct costs of treating these preventable cases—remains a critical evidence gap. Quantifying this 'dual burden' of health loss and financial cost is essential for policymakers to prioritize cost-effective prevention strategies, especially in resource-constrained settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the direct healthcare costs of treating cancers attributable to modifiable risk factors constitute a substantial and growing proportion of global oncology expenditure, and that the cost burden per case varies significantly by risk factor, cancer type, and healthcare system context.
proposals.method: We will extend the GBD 2023 comparative risk assessment framework. For each cancer type (e.g., lung, colorectal) in 204 countries and territories, we will combine GBD-estimated attributable case numbers with location-specific, phase-of-care cost estimates derived from literature review and databases like the WHO-CHOICE and International Agency for Research on Cancer's TARCC. Costs will be stratified by risk factor (e.g., tobacco, high BMI), age, sex, and year (1990–2023). We will employ spatial-temporal Gaussian process regression and ensemble models to interpolate cost data and propagate uncertainty from epidemiological and economic inputs.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first comprehensive, spatially-resolved estimates of the direct healthcare cost burden of preventable cancers, complementing existing DALY estimates. It will identify which risk factors drive the largest financial strain on health systems and how this economic burden is distributed globally, providing a crucial economic rationale for prevention investment.
proposals.novelty: While GBD quantifies health loss, and cost-of-illness studies exist, no study has systematically linked the full spectrum of modifiable cancer risks to direct treatment costs using a consistent, global comparative risk framework, creating a true 'dual burden' metric for policy evaluation.

ideas.title: Harmonizing Case Definitions: A Meta-Analytic Framework for Gout Prevalence
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that global disease prevalence can be directly compared when case definitions vary. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where the occurrence rate of exoplanets is estimated by correcting for the biases and detection limits of different survey methods, we propose a similar solution for gout epidemiology. We will develop a statistical meta-analytic framework that treats each country's prevalence data as originating from a 'survey' with a specific 'detection threshold' defined by its diagnostic criteria. By modeling the sensitivity and specificity of these criteria (e.g., ACR criteria, claims data algorithms, or hyperuricemia alone), the framework will apply completeness corrections to individual study estimates. The output will be a set of harmonized, bias-adjusted global and regional prevalence estimates for gout, explicitly quantifying how much variation is due to methodological differences versus true epidemiological variation.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Justice Mapping to Analyze Health Equity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the cumulative burden mapping solution from the field of Environmental Justice to analyze cause-specific mortality by socioeconomic strata. In environmental science, demographic and socioeconomic data are overlaid with pollution exposure data on maps to identify disadvantaged communities facing disproportionate environmental risks. The adapted research idea applies this geospatial-analytical framework to public health by creating high-resolution, subnational maps that overlay cause-specific mortality rates with multidimensional socioeconomic indicators (e.g., income, education, occupation, area deprivation indices). This approach would visually and quantitatively reveal 'mortality hotspots,' pinpointing the specific geographic and social strata where the burden from causes like cardiovascular disease, injuries, or specific cancers is most inequitably concentrated, thereby providing a powerful tool for targeting interventions and policies to reduce health disparities.

ideas.title: Adapting a Life-Course Epidemiology Framework to Disentangle Sex Disparity in Gout
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from cardiovascular disease epidemiology, where life-course frameworks successfully disentangled sex disparities in coronary heart disease. The analogous solution involved using longitudinal cohort data to model the accumulation and interaction of biological and behavioral risk factors across the lifespan for men and women separately. Adapted to gout, the idea is to implement a life-course epidemiology study design to investigate the drivers of the 3.26-fold higher male prevalence. This would involve integrating data from multiple existing cohorts to construct sex-specific trajectories of urate metabolism, renal function, hormonal status, and key behaviors from early adulthood onward. The goal is to move beyond simple case-control comparisons and identify critical periods, cumulative exposures, and effect modifiers that explain the divergent gout risk between sexes over time.

ideas.title: A Data-Assimilative Framework for Co-Occurring Mortality with Explicit Uncertainty from Imperfect Cause-of-Death Data
ideas.description: This research develops a modeling system that does not require perfect, granular cause-of-death data as a starting point. Instead, it uses a Bayesian hierarchical model to integrate fragmented, multi-resolution data sources (e.g., individual death records, aggregated population statistics, clinical studies) while rigorously quantifying uncertainty from misclassification and missing information. The framework incorporates known pathophysiological pathways of disease interaction as probabilistic constraints, allowing it to estimate the population-level burden and dynamics of co-occurring causes even with noisy, incomplete inputs. This makes the approach applicable and valid for real-world public health data where the ideal independent-categories assumption fails, but perfect joint-cause data is also unavailable.

ideas.title: Modeling Gout Burden as a Function of Multidimensional Socioeconomic Development Using Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecology's Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) to estimate gout burden. In ENMs, a species' distribution is predicted based on multivariate environmental conditions. The analogous solution is to model national gout prevalence and Years Lived with Disability (YLDs) not just by broad geographic region or simple income categories, but as a function of a multidimensional socioeconomic 'niche' defined by indices such as the Human Development Index (HDI), Gini coefficient, healthcare expenditure, urbanization, and specific dietary consumption data (e.g., purine-rich food availability, sugar-sweetened beverage sales). By applying multivariate statistical or machine learning techniques analogous to those used in ENMs, the research will identify the key socioeconomic drivers of gout burden and produce predictive models that map gout risk across a continuous gradient of national development, offering more nuanced, etiologically informative stratification for global health policy.

ideas.title: Causally Regularized Estimation of Non-Stationary Mortality Age Patterns with Competing Risks
ideas.description: This approach provides a robust framework for modeling shifts in the age distribution of deaths by integrating causal identification strategies and statistical regularization. It explicitly accounts for time-varying competing risks and cohort exposures to isolate the direct effect of interventions on cause-specific age-at-death distributions. The method uses penalized functional regression to estimate smooth, non-stationary age patterns while guarding against overfitting to noisy or sparse data. Finally, model selection is based on out-of-sample predictive performance, ensuring that identified temporal shifts are generalizable and epidemiologically plausible.

ideas.title: Adapting species distribution modeling to estimate subnational gout prevalence
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodological framework of Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) from Ecology to estimate subnational gout prevalence where health data are sparse. In ecology, SDMs combine limited species sighting data with high-resolution environmental layers (climate, vegetation) to predict geographic suitability. The analogous solution is adapted by treating sparse gout case reports from clinics or surveys as 'occurrence data' and using gout-relevant 'environmental' covariates—such as local dietary proxies, obesity estimates, and socioeconomic indicators derived from geospatial datasets—as predictors. The output is a probabilistic, small-area estimation map of gout prevalence risk, identifying high-burden subnational regions for targeted public health intervention, effectively transferring the SDM's solution for data-scarce spatial prediction to a non-communicable disease epidemiology context.

ideas.title: Geospatial Microsimulation for Equitable Gout Healthcare Resource Allocation
ideas.description: This research integrates small-area estimation techniques from poverty mapping with healthcare capacity forecasting to create a geospatial microsimulation framework. It uses spatially explicit covariates (e.g., diet, aging demographics, local risk factors) to downscale the projected 2050 global gout burden into high-resolution, subnational prevalence estimates. The model then links these localized prevalence forecasts with current healthcare infrastructure and workforce data to simulate future gaps in care capacity at a granular level. This enables the identification of specific regions at highest risk of being overwhelmed, guiding pre-emptive, evidence-based investment in workforce training and clinical infrastructure to improve equity and access.

It tests the mortality impact of realistic, scaled intervention portfolios—considering cost, delivery constraints, and synergistic or antagonistic effects between interventions. The output shifts from a theoretical 'attributable burden' to a time-phased projection of 'achievable burden reduction' under defined policy scenarios, providing a directly actionable tool for priority-setting and resource allocation.

ideas.title: Modeling Synergistic Survival Pathways: From Competing Risk to Co-operative Resilience
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the traditional competing risks model by focusing on how interacting causes of morbidity—typically studied for their joint contribution to death—can, in specific contexts, exhibit protective interactions or be mitigated by systemic resilience factors to produce exceptional survival. It seeks to identify populations, regions, or individual pathways where the co-occurrence of conditions (like a viral infection and a chronic disease) is associated with better-than-expected longevity outcomes, revealing latent survival mechanisms. The core methodology involves developing multi-state models that treat disease interactions as dynamic modifiers of survival trajectories, rather than just competing endpoints, to map networks of resilience. The ultimate goal is to shift public health strategy from siloed disease prevention to the proactive cultivation of systemic buffers and synergistic health supports that convert multi-morbidity from a mortality risk into a managed state with extended survival.

ideas.title: Model cause-specific mortality with time-varying age profiles using ecological niche shift methods
ideas.description: This idea challenges the stationary age-pattern assumption in mortality models by drawing an analogy from ecology, where species' geographic distributions shift over time due to environmental change. The analogous solution uses dynamic niche models to track these spatial shifts. Adapted to demography, we propose modeling the age distribution of deaths from a specific cause as a 'niche' within the age dimension that can shift and reshape over time. The methodological adaptation involves using flexible, time-parameterized functions to represent the mortality age profile, allowing its key features (like modal age, dispersion, and skew) to evolve. This enables the formal quantification of whether interventions or cohort effects are compressing, delaying, or broadening the age pattern of mortality from causes like cancer or dementia, providing a dynamic view of epidemiological change.

ideas.title: Modeling Shifting Epidemiological Patterns Using Climate Change Downscaling Techniques
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a stable age-sex pattern over time by drawing an analogy from climate science's solution to applying coarse global models to local contexts. In climate modeling, downscaling techniques are used to refine broad projections by incorporating local, time-varying factors. The adapted research idea applies this concept to epidemiological modeling. It proposes developing a statistical downscaling framework where core disease parameters (like male-to-female prevalence ratio or age of onset) are dynamically modulated by country-level developmental covariates (such as GDP, education, or sanitation access). This allows the demographic pattern of disease prevalence to shift non-stationarily in different future scenarios, moving beyond a fixed pattern to one that evolves with societal development.

ideas.title: Dynamic Ensemble Gout Forecasting with Real-Time Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This research establishes a dynamic forecasting platform for gout burden that integrates the multi-model ensemble uncertainty quantification from climate science with a continuous stream of real-world surveillance data. Instead of a one-time validation of 2050 projections, the system uses incoming incidence data from high-burden sentinel regions to iteratively weight, calibrate, and improve the constituent models within the ensemble. This creates a 'living forecast' where uncertainty bounds tighten and trajectory predictions become more accurate as new data is assimilated, directly informing adaptive public health interventions. The core innovation is the closed feedback loop between ensemble forecasting and real-time data, enabling proactive rather than reactive gout policy.

ideas.title: Modeling Co-Occurring Mortality Causes Using Ecological Niche and Interaction Theory
ideas.description: This research challenges the standard assumption of independent, mutually exclusive cause-of-death categories in mortality modeling. Drawing an analogy from community ecology, where species interactions are fundamental to population dynamics, the idea adapts ecological interaction frameworks (e.g., Lotka-Volterra competition, niche overlap) to the study of mortality. In this adapted model, different diseases or conditions (like COVID-19 and non-communicable diseases) are treated as interacting entities within the 'ecosystem' of an individual's health. The solution involves developing a mathematical model that quantifies interaction parameters (synergistic, competing, or conditional) between causes, allowing for the estimation of co-attributable mortality. This moves beyond simple comorbidity lists to a dynamic system where the combined force of interacting causes determines mortality risk, providing a more accurate representation of death processes, especially for complex multi-morbidity scenarios prevalent in aging populations and pandemics.

ideas.title: Individualized, Dynamic Risk Trajectory Modeling via Bayesian Agent-Based Simulation
ideas.description: This approach constructs a simulation where each agent (individual) is initialized with a unique, probabilistically coherent set of risk factors sampled from a Bayesian network informed by population data. The core innovation is that each agent's hazard for gout onset and progression events is not a fixed function of population parameters, but a dynamic function that updates based on the agent's personal history of biomarkers, comorbidities, and prior events. This creates truly heterogeneous, path-dependent lifetime trajectories. The model is then validated by ensuring the emergent population-level statistics from the ensemble of individual simulations recapitulate, and can potentially explain, observed epidemiological patterns.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Models to Stage-Specific Disability Weights in Gout
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a uniform disability weight for gout by drawing an analogy from ecology's handling of ecosystem disturbance. In ecology, the impact of an event is modeled dynamically across successional stages, each with its own metrics. The analogous solution involves mapping the temporal trajectory and assigning stage-specific values. Adapted to gout research, this means investigating if Years Lived with Disability (YLD) weights for gout vary by disease stage (acute flare vs. intercritical period vs. chronic tophaceous) or location. The study would treat gout progression analogously to ecological succession, using mixed-methods research to map the patient experience and functional impact across these distinct clinical stages to derive and propose multiple, context-specific disability weights for global burden of disease calculations.

ideas.description: This research integrates digital epidemiology (search/social media data) with dynamic demographic modeling to create a real-time surveillance system for gout. It uses the high-frequency, unstructured digital data to detect not just overall burden changes, but also early signals of shifts in the age-sex pattern of disease expression, such as a rising prevalence in younger adults or a narrowing gender gap. By fusing these streams, the model can continuously test and update its assumptions about demographic risk factors, providing a more agile and accurate picture of gout's evolving epidemiology in response to lifestyle, environmental, or healthcare changes.

ideas.title: Modeling a 'Metabolic Transition' to Invert the Primary Driver of Future Diabetes Case Growth
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the common assumption that future increases in diabetes cases will be driven primarily by population growth and aging. By drawing an analogy from climate science—specifically the modeling of carbon-climate feedbacks where warming itself becomes a major new source of greenhouse gases—we propose to model a 'metabolic transition' in public health. The analogous solution involves building systems dynamics models that incorporate strong feedback loops: where drastic increases in age-standardized diabetes prevalence (the analog to warming) lead to secondary effects like significant healthcare cost burdens, shifts in workforce productivity, and changes to the food environment. These effects then feedback to further accelerate the drivers of obesity and poor diet, potentially inverting the primary driver of case growth from simple demographic change to the prevalence rate itself. The goal is to model scenarios where this feedback loop becomes dominant, offering a starkly different and more urgent policy landscape than conventional projections.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution of Dietary Shifts on Age-Standardized Disease Burden Using Quasi-Experimental Frameworks
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlational scenario modeling to establish causal attribution. It designs a quasi-experimental system that identifies exogenous shocks or policy-driven variations in diet (e.g., sugar-sweetened beverage taxes, agricultural subsidies) to act as natural experiments. By applying difference-in-differences and instrumental variable techniques to linked health and consumption data, the system quantifies the causal contribution of specific dietary changes to future age-standardized disease prevalence. This isolates the effect from population aging and other confounders, providing robust, actionable evidence for whether diet can be a primary driver of case growth.

ideas.title: Positive Deviance Analysis of Longevity: Learning from Population 'Bright Spots'
ideas.description: This research seed inverts the focus from death to survival drivers. By drawing an analogy from the 'Positive Deviance' method in public health nutrition, the adapted idea proposes to study factors and regions with exceptional longevity gains. The analogous solution involves identifying resource-poor households with unexpectedly healthy children, uncovering their unique practices, and disseminating them. Adapted to longevity research, this means systematically identifying sub-national 'longevity bright spots'—communities that outperform their socioeconomic peers in life expectancy gains. Using mixed-methods, the research would uncover the specific behavioral, social, environmental, or healthcare organizational drivers behind this success to create transferable models for improving population health elsewhere, shifting the paradigm from analyzing failure to learning from success.

ideas.title: Applying Dark Matter Search Methods to Uncaptured Mortality Burden
ideas.description: This idea challenges the completeness of the standard 288-cause mortality taxonomy by drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where dark matter is detected through its gravitational effects rather than direct observation. The analogous solution involves inferring the presence and properties of an unseen entity (dark matter) by meticulously mapping discrepancies in observed phenomena (galactic rotations, gravitational lensing). Adapted to mortality research, the solution is to systematically analyze the residual patterns of death—demographic, geographic, and temporal—that are not accounted for by the existing 288 causes. By developing models to quantify and characterize this 'unexplained mortality burden,' the research would create an evidence-based map of emerging, poorly classified, or entirely missing causes, guiding future taxonomy revisions and surveillance priorities.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology, where causal forest algorithms are used to assess the heterogeneous effects of conservation policies in complex, non-randomized settings. The analogous solution involves using machine learning to model high-dimensional confounders and estimate conditional average treatment effects. Adapted to gout policy, this method would move beyond average impact estimates from difference-in-differences or synthetic control. By applying causal forests to administrative health data, the research would identify which specific patient populations benefited most or least from a policy intervention (like a medication access program), enabling more targeted and equitable future gout management strategies.

ideas.title: Refine subnational estimates using a hyper-resolution downscaling model
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from climate science, where coarse Global Climate Model outputs are downscaled to high-resolution local forecasts. The analogous problem in public health is having robust estimates for 811 subnational units but needing finer granularity for precise targeting. The adapted solution is to create a hyper-resolution downscaling model for mortality estimates. This model would treat the existing subnational estimates as the reliable but coarse boundary condition. It would then use a suite of high-resolution spatial covariates (such as population density from gridded datasets, satellite imagery, environmental factors, and accessibility metrics) in a Bayesian hierarchical framework to statistically disaggregate the estimates. The output would be a probabilistic mortality surface at a much finer spatial resolution (e.g., 1km²), enabling public health interventions to be targeted with unprecedented precision, similar to how hyper-local weather forecasts are generated for specific neighborhoods.

ideas.title: A multi-source data assimilation framework for dynamically constraining hyper-local gout burden microsimulations
ideas.description: This research designs a microsimulation system for gout burden that directly addresses the fatal data scarcity problem at hyper-local scales. It employs a dynamic Bayesian data assimilation framework to continuously integrate and weight diverse data streams, including traditional epidemiological sources and novel digital proxies for diet and treatment access, to constrain and update the model. This approach explicitly quantifies uncertainty and identifies which local data inputs most effectively reduce it, transforming the model from a static statistical downscaling exercise into an adaptive, evidence-integration tool. The result is a more robust, transparent, and utility-focused system for generating neighborhood-level estimates where uncertainty is managed and minimized through targeted data fusion.

ideas.title: Adaptive-resolution mortality estimation with uncertainty-guided spatial granularity
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond fixed high-resolution mapping by dynamically determining the appropriate spatial scale for each region based on data availability and statistical reliability. It employs hierarchical Bayesian models that share strength across neighboring areas while quantifying estimation uncertainty at multiple resolutions. The system outputs not just point estimates but explicit uncertainty boundaries, preventing over-interpretation of sparse data. Public health targeting is then optimized using these uncertainty-aware maps, ensuring interventions are directed where estimates are both precise and reliable.

ideas.title: Quantifying the uncaptured gout population using multi-source capture-recapture analysis
ideas.description: This study employs a three-source capture-recapture methodology to move beyond merely identifying the incompleteness of claims data and instead quantify the size of the undiagnosed/misdiagnosed gout population. By probabilistically linking de-identified patients across structured claims codes, unstructured EHR text analyzed via NLP for gout mentions, and validated patient surveys in a defined cohort, it estimates the total prevalence and the proportion missed by any single data source. The output provides a correction factor and confidence intervals that can be directly applied to claims-based epidemiological models to adjust for under-ascertainment, transforming a qualitative limitation into a quantifiable parameter.

ideas.title: Causally Robust Evaluation of Gout Policy via Confounder Discovery and Sensitivity Bounding
ideas.description: This research system first applies a suite of diagnostic and validation tests to rigorously assess the credibility of the parallel trends assumption before estimating a policy's impact. It employs placebo tests in pre-periods and on ineligible populations, alongside negative control outcomes, to detect hidden biases. The core analysis then proceeds with a difference-in-differences or synthetic control model, but is immediately followed by a formal sensitivity analysis that quantifies the robustness of the estimated effect to potential unobserved confounders. This end-to-end approach provides a transparent, bounded estimate of the policy's impact, clearly stating the evidence for causality and the conditions under which the conclusion might fail.

ideas.title: Microsimulation of Gout Burden Using Agent-Based Urban Ecology Models
ideas.description: This research adapts agent-based modeling solutions from urban ecology to estimate hyper-local gout burden. In ecology, agent-based models simulate individual organisms in a patchy urban landscape to predict population outcomes. Here, we construct a spatially explicit microsimulation where individual 'person-agents' reside in specific city neighborhoods. Each agent has a profile of risk factors (age, sex, genetics, diet, renal function, medication use) informed by local data. The agents interact with a quantified neighborhood environment (e.g., density of purine-rich food outlets, green space for exercise, proximity to primary care). Through stochastic simulation of urate metabolism, crystal formation, flare triggers, and disease progression over time, the model generates estimates of gout prevalence, flare frequency, and associated disability (e.g., quality-adjusted life years lost) for each micro-geography. This provides a powerful tool for identifying neighborhood-level disparities and targeting public health interventions.

ideas.title: Identifying Protective Factors in Gout-Resistant Populations via a Cancer Biology Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from cancer biology, where studying populations resistant to cancer despite high risk has revealed protective factors. The analogous problem is identifying regions or populations with unexpectedly low gout prevalence given their high-risk profile (e.g., high purine diet, metabolic syndrome). The adapted idea is to conduct a case-control study comparing these 'gout-resistant' high-risk populations with matched 'gout-susceptible' high-risk populations. By employing techniques from the analogous field—such as comparative genomics for protective alleles, metagenomic sequencing for protective gut flora, and metabolomic profiling for identifying urate-lowering or anti-inflammatory metabolites—the research aims to discover novel biological and environmental mechanisms of protection against gout, which could inform new preventive strategies and therapeutics.

ideas.title: Causal pathway analysis to position gout within dynamic multimorbidity networks
ideas.description: This research first deconstructs the assumed relationship between gout and cardiometabolic diseases by rigorously testing competing causal hypotheses using longitudinal data and genetic instruments. It applies causal inference methods, such as Mendelian Randomization and cross-lagged modeling, to establish the directionality and strength of temporal relationships between gout onset, urate levels, and incident cardiovascular/metabolic conditions. The validated causal pathways then inform the construction of a dynamic, mechanistic multimorbidity model where gout is embedded not as a static component, but as a node with defined causal influence (driver, consequence, or amplifier) within the disease network. This approach ensures clinical models are biologically plausible and directly identify high-value targets for intervention within the gout-cardiometabolic disease interface.

ideas.title: Epidemiological Load-Balancing: Adapting Power Grid Stability Models to Forecast Gout Healthcare System Requirements
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from electrical power grid management to forecast healthcare capacity for gout. In power systems, engineers use dynamic load-flow models to predict demand, stress-test infrastructure, and plan expansions to prevent blackouts. Analogously, we propose modeling the future healthcare system as a network where the projected 95.8 million gout cases in 2050 represent a surging 'demand load.' The model will map patient flow through care nodes (e.g., GPs, specialists, hospitals), each with defined workforce and infrastructure 'capacity.' By simulating this epidemiological load, we can identify system bottlenecks (equivalent to overloaded transformers), forecast points of service failure, and test the efficacy of different intervention strategies—such as workforce training, telemedicine integration, or facility expansion—to 'reinforce the grid' and ensure the healthcare system remains stable and accessible under future demand.

ideas.title: Adapting Data Fusion Techniques from Seismology for Multi-Source Mortality Estimation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from seismology, where data fusion techniques integrate sparse traditional sensor data with novel streams like fiber-optic DAS and smartphone networks to map seismic events. The analogous solution involves using advanced statistical models to combine these heterogeneous, noisy data sources. Adapted to mortality estimation, the research would develop a computational framework to fuse underutilized digital health records with other novel streams—such as aggregated pharmacy sales data, mobile health worker reports, and syndromic surveillance from search trends—using hierarchical Bayesian and machine learning methods derived from geophysical inverse problem-solving. This approach would create a robust, multi-source estimation model that improves accuracy in data-sparse regions by explicitly handling the variability, bias, and incompleteness of each integrated data stream.

ideas.title: Adapt Network Ecology Methods to Model Gout as a Keystone Node in Multimorbidity Networks
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the problem of scaling up to integrate gout into multimorbidity models by drawing an analogy from network ecology. In that field, the problem of understanding a species' systemic impact is solved by constructing interaction networks and using keystone species analysis. We adapt this by building multimorbidity networks from longitudinal health data, where nodes are diseases (including gout, CVD, diabetes, CKD) and edges represent shared risk factors, temporal progression, or co-management links. Using centrality metrics (e.g., betweenness, eigenvector), we can empirically determine if gout occupies a keystone position, driving connectivity between metabolic and cardiovascular disease modules. This provides a quantitative, mechanistic framework to embed gout not just as another comorbidity, but as a potential integrator and amplifier within larger multimorbidity models.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismology's Aftershock Detection to Gout Flare Surveillance
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from seismology, where networks of sensors detect primary seismic waves to warn of impending shaking. The analogous problem in digital epidemiology is predicting gout flare outbreaks from early, often subtle, digital signals. The adapted research idea treats patient-generated digital data (social media posts, search queries describing prodromal symptoms) as seismic sensors. By applying pattern recognition and predictive algorithms similar to those used in earthquake early warning systems, we can develop a model to detect clusters of early symptom signals in real-time. This model would forecast the location, scale, and timing of subsequent surges in clinically significant gout flare burden, transforming gout surveillance from reactive reporting to proactive prediction for public health and clinical management.
